There are no surrounding {{ill|tamagaki|ja|玉垣|12=draft}} fences or offering boxes.
== Enshrined deity ==
The enshrined deity is Mishine-no-Mikura-no-Kami (御稲御倉神)<ref name="ic"/>. This deity serves as the {{ill|guardian deity|ja|守護神|12=draft}} of Mishine-no-Mikura<ref name="ic"/><ref name="gk"/>. During the {{ill|Jingū Shikinen Sengū|ja|神宮式年遷宮|12=draft}}, when the shrine is rebuilt, Mishine-no-Mikura-no-Kami is temporarily transferred to the Geheiden<ref>Ise Bunka-sha (2013): p.6</ref>.
In the Heian-period text "Daiji Mikagami," the deity is referred to as {{ill|Ukemochi-no-Kami|ja|保食神|12=draft}}, while in the Kamakura-period "Jinmyō Hisho," it is identified as {{ill|Ukanomitama|ja|ウカノミタマ|12=draft}}<ref name="gs P155-156">Sakurai (1970): pp.155–156</ref>.
== History ==
It is believed to have existed since ancient times<ref name="gs"/>. Originally, it was part of a complex of four granaries—Tsuki-no-Mikura, Mishio-no-Mikura, Fushiki-no-Mikura, and Mishine-no-Mikura—located west of the outer fence. By the mid-Heian period, these granaries, along with the Geheiden, were moved inside the outer sacred fence<ref>Fukuyama et al. (1975): pp.228–229</ref>. At the time, the structure was built in the {{ill|azekura-zukuri|ja|校倉造|12=draft}} style without chigi and katsuogi<ref name="ss9">Fukuyama et al. (1975): p.229</ref>. As it was within the grounds of the main sanctuary, it was relocated with each Shikinen Sengū, but all four granaries were abolished by the end of the medieval period<ref name="ss9"/>.
The rice retrieval ceremony was conducted by four officials, including {{ill|negi|ja|禰宜|12=draft}} and Ōuchi-hito, accompanied by Ōmonoimi (great purification officials)<ref name="gs"/>. Their attire consisted of the same sacred garments used during Naikū rituals, indicating the shrine's significance<ref name="gs"/>. The retrieved rice was milled, steamed, and served in sacred vessels ({{ill|ke|ja|笥|12=draft}}) before the deities<ref name="gs2"/>. The offerings were dedicated to Naikū, {{ill|Aramatsuri-no-Miya|ja|荒祭宮|12=draft}}, and {{ill|Takimatsuri-no-Kami|ja|滝deity|12=draft}}<ref name="gs2"/>.
During the {{ill|Tenshō era|ja|天正|1=Tenshō (Momoyama period)|lt=Tenshō Era|12=draft}} (1573–1592), Mishine-no-Mikura was restored in the Yuitsu-shinmei-zukuri style northwest of the main sanctuary and has since been rebuilt with each Shikinen Sengū<ref name="ss9"/>. After the Muromachi period, it was also known as the "Sacred Weaving Hall" (御機殿) due to its association with sacred weaving during the {{ill|Kannamesai|ja|神嘗祭|12=draft}}<ref name="ss9"/>.
Since the Meiji period, Mishine-no-Mikura-no-Kami has been formally enshrined, and in 1889, the shrine’s location was fixed along the path leading to Aramatsuri-no-Miya<ref name="ss9"/>.
== Access ==
; To Ujibashi Bridge * About 15 minutes by {{ill|Mie Kotsu|ja|三重交通|12=draft}} bus from the south exit (JR side) of {{ill|Ise Station|ja|伊勢市駅|12=draft}} on the {{ill|JR Sangū Line|ja|参宮線|12=draft}} or from {{ill|Ujiyamada Station|ja|宇治山田駅|12=draft}} on the Kintetsu Yamada and Toba Lines, alighting at the final stop "Naikū-mae"<ref name="mkr">Mie Prefecture Tourism Federation, "[http://www.kankomie.or.jp/spot/detail_2937.html Ise Jingu (Naikū) Tourist Info - Visit Mie]" (Accessed October 12, 2013)</ref>. Also accessible via bus from {{ill|Isuzugawa Station|ja|五十鈴川駅|12=draft}} on the Kintetsu Toba Line. * About 5 minutes south via {{ill|Mie Prefectural Route 32|ja|三重県道32号伊勢磯部線|12=draft}} (Mikimoto Road) from {{ill|Ise-nishi Interchange|ja|伊勢西インターチェンジ|12=draft}} on the {{ill|Ise Expressway|ja|伊勢自動車道|12=draft}}, or from {{ill|Ise Interchange|ja|伊勢インターチェンジ|12=draft}} via {{ill|National Route 23 (Japan)|ja|国道23号|12=draft}}. Parking available nearby<ref name="mkr"/>.
; From Ujibashi Bridge * Proceed along the path from Ujibashi toward the main sanctuary of Naikū, then take the path leading west toward Aramatsuri-no-Miya, where Mishine-no-Mikura is enshrined<ref name="ic"/>. The nearby Geheiden can also be found here<ref name="ic"/>.

{{Shrine |Name = Mishine no Mikura |religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Mishine no Mikura.jpg|270px]] |location = {{ill|Mie Prefecture|ja|三重県|12=draft}}, {{ill|Ise, Mie|ja|伊勢市|12=draft}}, Ujikan-cho 1, within the precincts of the Inner Shrine |Latitude Degrees = 34 | Latitude Minutes = 27 | Latitude Seconds = 18.8 |Longitude Degrees = 136 | Longitude Minutes = 43 | Longitude Seconds = 28.7 |Map = Japan Mie |Label Position = bottom |deity = Mishine no Mikura no Kami |type = {{ill|Kōtai Jingū|ja|皇大神宮|1=Kōtai Jingū|2=ja|3=皇大神宮|4=zh|5=皇大神宮|12=draft}} auxiliary shrine |Established = Ancient times |Main Hall = {{ill|Yuitsu Shimmei-zukuri|ja|唯一神明造|12=draft}} |Other Names = |Pilgrimage Sites = |festival = |Rituals = {{ill|Kannamesai|ja|神嘗祭|12=draft}} }}
'''Mishine no Mikura''' (御稲御倉, also read as *Gotō no Mikura*<ref name="ss8">Fukuyama et al. (1975): p. 228</ref>) is an auxiliary shrine under the jurisdiction of {{ill|Kōtai Jingū|ja|皇大神宮|1=Kōtai Jingū|2=ja|3=皇大神宮|4=zh|5=皇大神宮|12=draft}} (the Inner Shrine) of {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮|12=draft}}. It is also referred to as '''Mishine no Mikura no Kami''' (御稲御倉神)<ref name="ic">Ise Bunka-sha, ed. (2008): p. 114</ref><ref>Ujiyamada City Office, ed. (1929): p. 16</ref><ref name="gs2">Sakurai (1970): p. 156</ref>. The shrine is located within the precincts of the Inner Shrine, along the path leading to {{ill|Aramatsuri-no-Miya|ja|荒祭宮|12=draft}}<ref name="ic"/>.
Although small, it features the same {{ill|Yuitsu Shimmei-zukuri|ja|唯一神明造|12=draft}} architectural style as the Inner Shrine<ref name="ic"/><ref name="gk">Gakken Publishing (2013): p. 56</ref>.
== Overview ==
Located in {{ill|Ujikanda-chō|ja|宇治館町|12=draft}}, {{ill|Ise City|ja|伊勢市|12=draft}}, Mie Prefecture, this shrine stands northwest of the main sanctuary (Shōgu) within the precincts of the {{ill|Naikū|ja|内宮|12=draft}}<ref name="gk"/>. It ranks 6th among the 30 shrines under the jurisdiction of Naikū. Each time a festival is held at the main sanctuary of Naikū, a ritual is also conducted at the {{ill|Mishine-no-Mikura|ja|御稲御倉|12=draft}}<ref name="ic"/>. To the north of Mishine-no-Mikura is the {{ill|Geheiden|ja|外幣殿|12=draft}}<ref name="ss8"/>.
The {{ill|rice|ja|イネ|12=draft}} harvested from the sacred fields of Ise Jingu is presented as {{ill|nuiho|ja|抜穂|12=draft}} and stored in Mishine-no-Mikura<ref name="ic"/>. This rice is later retrieved in accordance with Naikū’s rituals and offered as {{ill|Ōmikue|ja|大御饌|12=draft}} to {{ill|Amaterasu|ja|天照大神|12=draft}}<ref name="ic"/><ref name="gk"/>. Essentially, it functions as the granary of Naikū<ref name="gk"/>. Following ancient customs, Shinto priests dressed in traditional garments deliver the rice to the Mikura<ref name="gs">Sakurai (1970): p.155</ref>.
The act of retrieving rice from the Mikura is called either {{ill|Gotō Hōge|ja|御稲奉下|12=draft}} (Gotō Hōge<ref name="gs"/>, Mishine Hōge<ref name="sy">Yano (2006): p.111</ref>). It is carried out by priests who have undergone purification rites the day before<ref name="gs"/>. After retrieval, the rice undergoes threshing, dehusking, and polishing to about 70%. It is then prepared into {{ill|sacred sake|ja|神酒|12=draft}}, {{ill|mochi|ja|餅|12=draft}}, and cooked rice ({{ill|Onii|ja|御飯|12=draft}}) in the {{ill|Imibiya-den|ja|忌火屋殿|12=draft}}, and offered before the deities<ref name="sy"/>.
Additionally, during the {{ill|Jingū Shikinen Sengū|ja|神宮式年遷宮|12=draft}}, one of the key rituals involves the placement of the central sacred pillar ({{ill|Shin-no-Mihashira|ja|心御柱|12=draft}}), which is temporarily stored in Mishine-no-Mikura<ref>{{Cite news|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20130926070226/http://sankei.jp.msn.com/life/news/130925/trd13092522350017-n1.htm|title=Shin-no-Mihashira Ceremony at Naikū for Jingū Shikinen Sengū|publisher=Sankei Shimbun|work=MSN Sankei News|date=September 25, 2013|accessdate=October 13, 2013}}</ref>.
The shrine building is constructed in the {{ill|Yuitsu-shinmei-zukuri|ja|唯一神明造|12=draft}} style<ref name="ic"/>. It faces east toward Naikū<ref name="ic"/>. The elevated structure with a {{ill|kirizuma-style|ja|切妻|12=draft}} roof and decorative {{ill|chigi and katsuogi|ja|千木・鰹木|12=draft}} resembles the East and West Treasuries of Naikū and the Geheiden<ref name="ss8"/>. Due to its elevated design, a removable staircase is attached during rice retrieval. The {{ill|Gon-negi|ja|権禰宜|12=draft}} unlocks the door with a sacred key ({{ill|Mikagi|ja|御鑰|12=draft}}) to access the rice<ref>Yano (2006): pp.111–112</ref>. There are no surrounding {{ill|tamagaki|ja|玉垣|12=draft}} fences or offering boxes.
== Enshrined deity ==
The enshrined deity is Mishine-no-Mikura-no-Kami (御稲御倉神)<ref name="ic"/>. This deity serves as the {{ill|guardian deity|ja|守護神|12=draft}} of Mishine-no-Mikura<ref name="ic"/><ref name="gk"/>. During the {{ill|Jingū Shikinen Sengū|ja|神宮式年遷宮|12=draft}}, when the shrine is rebuilt, Mishine-no-Mikura-no-Kami is temporarily transferred to the Geheiden<ref>Ise Bunka-sha (2013): p.6</ref>.
In the Heian-period text "Daiji Mikagami," the deity is referred to as {{ill|Ukemochi-no-Kami|ja|保食神|12=draft}}, while in the Kamakura-period "Jinmyō Hisho," it is identified as {{ill|Ukanomitama|ja|ウカノミタマ|12=draft}}<ref name="gs P155-156">Sakurai (1970): pp.155–156</ref>.
== History ==
It is believed to have existed since ancient times<ref name="gs"/>. Originally, it was part of a complex of four granaries—Tsuki-no-Mikura, Mishio-no-Mikura, Fushiki-no-Mikura, and Mishine-no-Mikura—located west of the outer fence. By the mid-Heian period, these granaries, along with the Geheiden, were moved inside the outer sacred fence<ref>Fukuyama et al. (1975): pp.228–229</ref>. At the time, the structure was built in the {{ill|azekura-zukuri|ja|校倉造|12=draft}} style without chigi and katsuogi<ref name="ss9">Fukuyama et al. (1975): p.229</ref>. As it was within the grounds of the main sanctuary, it was relocated with each Shikinen Sengū, but all four granaries were abolished by the end of the medieval period<ref name="ss9"/>.
The rice retrieval ceremony was conducted by four officials, including {{ill|negi|ja|禰宜|12=draft}} and Ōuchi-hito, accompanied by Ōmonoimi (great purification officials)<ref name="gs"/>. Their attire consisted of the same sacred garments used during Naikū rituals, indicating the shrine's significance<ref name="gs"/>. The retrieved rice was milled, steamed, and served in sacred vessels ({{ill|ke|ja|笥|12=draft}}) before the deities<ref name="gs2"/>. The offerings were dedicated to Naikū, {{ill|Aramatsuri-no-Miya|ja|荒祭宮|12=draft}}, and {{ill|Takimatsuri-no-Kami|ja|滝deity|12=draft}}<ref name="gs2"/>.
During the {{ill|Tenshō era|ja|天正|1=Tenshō (Momoyama period)|lt=Tenshō Era|12=draft}} (1573–1592), Mishine-no-Mikura was restored in the Yuitsu-shinmei-zukuri style northwest of the main sanctuary and has since been rebuilt with each Shikinen Sengū<ref name="ss9"/>. After the Muromachi period, it was also known as the "Sacred Weaving Hall" (御機殿) due to its association with sacred weaving during the {{ill|Kannamesai|ja|神嘗祭|12=draft}}<ref name="ss9"/>.
Since the Meiji period, Mishine-no-Mikura-no-Kami has been formally enshrined, and in 1889, the shrine’s location was fixed along the path leading to Aramatsuri-no-Miya<ref name="ss9"/>.

; To Ujibashi Bridge * About 15 minutes by {{ill|Mie Kotsu|ja|三重交通|12=draft}} bus from the south exit (JR side) of {{ill|Ise Station|ja|伊勢市駅|12=draft}} on the {{ill|JR Sangū Line|ja|参宮線|12=draft}} or from {{ill|Ujiyamada Station|ja|宇治山田駅|12=draft}} on the Kintetsu Yamada and Toba Lines, alighting at the final stop "Naikū-mae"<ref name="mkr">Mie Prefecture Tourism Federation, "[http://www.kankomie.or.jp/spot/detail_2937.html Ise Jingu (Naikū) Tourist Info - Visit Mie]" (Accessed October 12, 2013)</ref>. Also accessible via bus from {{ill|Isuzugawa Station|ja|五十鈴川駅|12=draft}} on the Kintetsu Toba Line. * About 5 minutes south via {{ill|Mie Prefectural Route 32|ja|三重県道32号伊勢磯部線|12=draft}} (Mikimoto Road) from {{ill|Ise-nishi Interchange|ja|伊勢西インターチェンジ|12=draft}} on the {{ill|Ise Expressway|ja|伊勢自動車道|12=draft}}, or from {{ill|Ise Interchange|ja|伊勢インターチェンジ|12=draft}} via {{ill|National Route 23 (Japan)|ja|国道23号|12=draft}}. Parking available nearby<ref name="mkr"/>.
; From Ujibashi Bridge * Proceed along the path from Ujibashi toward the main sanctuary of Naikū, then take the path leading west toward Aramatsuri-no-Miya, where Mishine-no-Mikura is enshrined<ref name="ic"/>. The nearby Geheiden can also be found here<ref name="ic"/>.

It is carried out by priests who have undergone purification rites the day before<ref name="gs"/>. After retrieval, the rice undergoes threshing, dehusking, and polishing to about 70%. It is then prepared into {{ill|sacred sake|ja|神酒|12=draft}}, {{ill|mochi|ja|餅|12=draft}}, and cooked rice ({{ill|Onii|ja|御飯|12=draft}}) in the {{ill|Imibiya-den|ja|忌火屋殿|12=draft}}, and offered before the deities<ref name="sy"/>.
Additionally, during the {{ill|Jingū Shikinen Sengū|ja|神宮式年遷宮|12=draft}}, one of the key rituals involves the placement of the central sacred pillar ({{ill|Shin-no-Mihashira|ja|心御柱|12=draft}}), which is temporarily stored in Mishine-no-Mikura<ref>{{Cite news|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20130926070226/http://sankei.jp.msn.com/life/news/130925/trd13092522350017-n1.htm|title=Shin-no-Mihashira Ceremony at Naikū for Jingū Shikinen Sengū|publisher=Sankei Shimbun|work=MSN Sankei News|date=September 25, 2013|accessdate=October 13, 2013}}</ref>.
The shrine building is constructed in the {{ill|Yuitsu-shinmei-zukuri|ja|唯一神明造|12=draft}} style<ref name="ic"/>. It faces east toward Naikū<ref name="ic"/>. The elevated structure with a {{ill|kirizuma-style|ja|切妻|12=draft}} roof and decorative {{ill|chigi and katsuogi|ja|千木・鰹木|12=draft}} resembles the East and West Treasuries of Naikū and the Geheiden<ref name="ss8"/>. Due to its elevated design, a removable staircase is attached during rice retrieval. The {{ill|Gon-negi|ja|権禰宜|12=draft}} unlocks the door with a sacred key ({{ill|Mikagi|ja|御鑰|12=draft}}) to access the rice<ref>Yano (2006): pp.111–112</ref>. There are no surrounding {{ill|tamagaki|ja|玉垣|12=draft}} fences or offering boxes.
== Enshrined deity ==
The enshrined deity is Mishine-no-Mikura-no-Kami (御稲御倉神)<ref name="ic"/>. This deity serves as the {{ill|guardian deity|ja|守護神|12=draft}} of Mishine-no-Mikura<ref name="ic"/><ref name="gk"/>. During the {{ill|Jingū Shikinen Sengū|ja|神宮式年遷宮|12=draft}}, when the shrine is rebuilt, Mishine-no-Mikura-no-Kami is temporarily transferred to the Geheiden<ref>Ise Bunka-sha (2013): p.6</ref>.
In the Heian-period text "Daiji Mikagami," the deity is referred to as {{ill|Ukemochi-no-Kami|ja|保食神|12=draft}}, while in the Kamakura-period "Jinmyō Hisho," it is identified as {{ill|Ukanomitama|ja|ウカノミタマ|12=draft}}<ref name="gs P155-156">Sakurai (1970): pp.155–156</ref>.
== History ==
It is believed to have existed since ancient times<ref name="gs"/>. Originally, it was part of a complex of four granaries—Tsuki-no-Mikura, Mishio-no-Mikura, Fushiki-no-Mikura, and Mishine-no-Mikura—located west of the outer fence. By the mid-Heian period, these granaries, along with the Geheiden, were moved inside the outer sacred fence<ref>Fukuyama et al. (1975): pp.228–229</ref>. At the time, the structure was built in the {{ill|azekura-zukuri|ja|校倉造|12=draft}} style without chigi and katsuogi<ref name="ss9">Fukuyama et al. (1975): p.229</ref>. As it was within the grounds of the main sanctuary, it was relocated with each Shikinen Sengū, but all four granaries were abolished by the end of the medieval period<ref name="ss9"/>.
The rice retrieval ceremony was conducted by four officials, including {{ill|negi|ja|禰宜|12=draft}} and Ōuchi-hito, accompanied by Ōmonoimi (great purification officials)<ref name="gs"/>. Their attire consisted of the same sacred garments used during Naikū rituals, indicating the shrine's significance<ref name="gs"/>. The retrieved rice was milled, steamed, and served in sacred vessels ({{ill|ke|ja|笥|12=draft}}) before the deities<ref name="gs2"/>. The offerings were dedicated to Naikū, {{ill|Aramatsuri-no-Miya|ja|荒祭宮|12=draft}}, and {{ill|Takimatsuri-no-Kami|ja|滝deity|12=draft}}<ref name="gs2"/>.
During the {{ill|Tenshō era|ja|天正|1=Tenshō (Momoyama period)|lt=Tenshō Era|12=draft}} (1573–1592), Mishine-no-Mikura was restored in the Yuitsu-shinmei-zukuri style northwest of the main sanctuary and has since been rebuilt with each Shikinen Sengū<ref name="ss9"/>. After the Muromachi period, it was also known as the "Sacred Weaving Hall" (御機殿) due to its association with sacred weaving during the {{ill|Kannamesai|ja|神嘗祭|12=draft}}<ref name="ss9"/>.
Since the Meiji period, Mishine-no-Mikura-no-Kami has been formally enshrined, and in 1889, the shrine’s location was fixed along the path leading to Aramatsuri-no-Miya<ref name="ss9"/>.
== Access ==
; To Ujibashi Bridge * About 15 minutes by {{ill|Mie Kotsu|ja|三重交通|12=draft}} bus from the south exit (JR side) of {{ill|Ise Station|ja|伊勢市駅|12=draft}} on the {{ill|JR Sangū Line|ja|参宮線|12=draft}} or from {{ill|Ujiyamada Station|ja|宇治山田駅|12=draft}} on the Kintetsu Yamada and Toba Lines, alighting at the final stop "Naikū-mae"<ref name="mkr">Mie Prefecture Tourism Federation, "[http://www.kankomie.or.jp/spot/detail_2937.html Ise Jingu (Naikū) Tourist Info - Visit Mie]" (Accessed October 12, 2013)</ref>. Also accessible via bus from {{ill|Isuzugawa Station|ja|五十鈴川駅|12=draft}} on the Kintetsu Toba Line. * About 5 minutes south via {{ill|Mie Prefectural Route 32|ja|三重県道32号伊勢磯部線|12=draft}} (Mikimoto Road) from {{ill|Ise-nishi Interchange|ja|伊勢西インターチェンジ|12=draft}} on the {{ill|Ise Expressway|ja|伊勢自動車道|12=draft}}, or from {{ill|Ise Interchange|ja|伊勢インターチェンジ|12=draft}} via {{ill|National Route 23 (Japan)|ja|国道23号|12=draft}}. Parking available nearby<ref name="mkr"/>.
; From Ujibashi Bridge * Proceed along the path from Ujibashi toward the main sanctuary of Naikū, then take the path leading west toward Aramatsuri-no-Miya, where Mishine-no-Mikura is enshrined<ref name="ic"/>. The nearby Geheiden can also be found here<ref name="ic"/>.

{{Shrine |Name = Mishine no Mikura |religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Mishine no Mikura.jpg|270px]] |location = {{ill|Mie Prefecture|ja|三重県|12=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|Ise, Mie|ja|伊勢市|12=draft|12=draft}}, Ujikan-cho 1, within the precincts of the Inner Shrine |Latitude Degrees = 34 | Latitude Minutes = 27 | Latitude Seconds = 18.8 |Longitude Degrees = 136 | Longitude Minutes = 43 | Longitude Seconds = 28.7 |Map = Japan Mie |Label Position = bottom |deity = Mishine no Mikura no Kami |type = {{ill|Kōtai Jingū|ja|皇大神宮|1=Kōtai Jingū|2=ja|3=皇大神宮|4=zh|5=皇大神宮|12=draft|12=draft}} auxiliary shrine |Established = Ancient times |Main Hall = {{ill|Yuitsu Shimmei-zukuri|ja|唯一神明造|12=draft|12=draft}} |Other Names = |Pilgrimage Sites = |festival = |Rituals = {{ill|Kannamesai|ja|神嘗祭|12=draft|12=draft}} }}
'''Mishine no Mikura''' (御稲御倉, also read as *Gotō no Mikura*<ref name="ss8">Fukuyama et al. (1975): p. 228</ref>) is an auxiliary shrine under the jurisdiction of {{ill|Kōtai Jingū|ja|皇大神宮|1=Kōtai Jingū|2=ja|3=皇大神宮|4=zh|5=皇大神宮|12=draft|12=draft}} (the Inner Shrine) of {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮|12=draft|12=draft}}. It is also referred to as '''Mishine no Mikura no Kami''' (御稲御倉神)<ref name="ic">Ise Bunka-sha, ed. (2008): p. 114</ref><ref>Ujiyamada City Office, ed. (1929): p. 16</ref><ref name="gs2">Sakurai (1970): p. 156</ref>. The shrine is located within the precincts of the Inner Shrine, along the path leading to {{ill|Aramatsuri-no-Miya|ja|荒祭宮|12=draft|12=draft}}<ref name="ic"/>.
Although small, it features the same {{ill|Yuitsu Shimmei-zukuri|ja|唯一神明造|12=draft|12=draft}} architectural style as the Inner Shrine<ref name="ic"/><ref name="gk">Gakken Publishing (2013): p. 56</ref>.
== Overview ==
Located in {{ill|Ujikanda-chō|ja|宇治館町|12=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|Ise City|ja|伊勢市|12=draft|12=draft}}, Mie Prefecture, this shrine stands northwest of the main sanctuary (Shōgu) within the precincts of the {{ill|Naikū|ja|内宮|12=draft|12=draft}}<ref name="gk"/>. It ranks 6th among the 30 shrines under the jurisdiction of Naikū. Each time a festival is held at the main sanctuary of Naikū, a ritual is also conducted at the {{ill|Mishine-no-Mikura|ja|御稲御倉|12=draft|12=draft}}<ref name="ic"/>. To the north of Mishine-no-Mikura is the {{ill|Geheiden|ja|外幣殿|12=draft|12=draft}}<ref name="ss8"/>.
The {{ill|rice|ja|イネ|12=draft|12=draft}} harvested from the sacred fields of Ise Jingu is presented as {{ill|nuiho|ja|抜穂|12=draft|12=draft}} and stored in Mishine-no-Mikura<ref name="ic"/>. This rice is later retrieved in accordance with Naikū’s rituals and offered as {{ill|Ōmikue|ja|大御饌|12=draft|12=draft}} to {{ill|Amaterasu|ja|天照大神|1=Amaterasu|lt=Amaterasu Ōmikami|12=draft|12=draft}}<ref name="ic"/><ref name="gk"/>. Essentially, it functions as the granary of Naikū<ref name="gk"/>. Following ancient customs, Shinto priests dressed in traditional garments deliver the rice to the Mikura<ref name="gs">Sakurai (1970): p.155</ref>.
The act of retrieving rice from the Mikura is called either {{ill|Gotō Hōge|ja|御稲奉下|12=draft|12=draft}} (Gotō Hōge<ref name="gs"/>, Mishine Hōge<ref name="sy">Yano (2006): p.111</ref>). It is carried out by priests who have undergone purification rites the day before<ref name="gs"/>. After retrieval, the rice undergoes threshing, dehusking, and polishing to about 70%. It is then prepared into {{ill|sacred sake|ja|神酒|12=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|mochi|ja|餅|12=draft|12=draft}}, and cooked rice ({{ill|Onii|ja|御飯|12=draft|12=draft}}) in the {{ill|Imibiya-den|ja|忌火屋殿|12=draft|12=draft}}, and offered before the deities<ref name="sy"/>.
Additionally, during the {{ill|Jingū Shikinen Sengū|ja|神宮式年遷宮|12=draft|12=draft}}, one of the key rituals involves the placement of the central sacred pillar ({{ill|Shin-no-Mihashira|ja|心御柱|12=draft|12=draft}}), which is temporarily stored in Mishine-no-Mikura<ref>{{Cite news|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20130926070226/http://sankei.jp.msn.com/life/news/130925/trd13092522350017-n1.htm|title=Shin-no-Mihashira Ceremony at Naikū for Jingū Shikinen Sengū|publisher=Sankei Shimbun|work=MSN Sankei News|date=September 25, 2013|accessdate=October 13, 2013}}</ref>.
The shrine building is constructed in the {{ill|Yuitsu-shinmei-zukuri|ja|唯一神明造|12=draft|12=draft}} style<ref name="ic"/>. It faces east toward Naikū<ref name="ic"/>. The elevated structure with a {{ill|kirizuma-style|ja|切妻|12=draft|12=draft}} roof and decorative {{ill|chigi and katsuogi|ja|千木・鰹木|12=draft|12=draft}} resembles the East and West Treasuries of Naikū and the Geheiden<ref name="ss8"/>. Due to its elevated design, a removable staircase is attached during rice retrieval. The {{ill|Gon-negi|ja|権禰宜|12=draft|12=draft}} unlocks the door with a sacred key ({{ill|Mikagi|ja|御鑰|12=draft|12=draft}}) to access the rice<ref>Yano (2006): pp.111–112</ref>. There are no surrounding {{ill|tamagaki|ja|玉垣|12=draft|12=draft}} fences or offering boxes.
== Enshrined deity ==
The enshrined deity is Mishine-no-Mikura-no-Kami (御稲御倉神)<ref name="ic"/>. This deity serves as the {{ill|guardian deity|ja|守護神|12=draft|12=draft}} of Mishine-no-Mikura<ref name="ic"/><ref name="gk"/>. During the {{ill|Jingū Shikinen Sengū|ja|神宮式年遷宮|12=draft|12=draft}}, when the shrine is rebuilt, Mishine-no-Mikura-no-Kami is temporarily transferred to the Geheiden<ref>Ise Bunka-sha (2013): p.6</ref>.
In the Heian-period text "Daiji Mikagami," the deity is referred to as {{ill|Ukemochi-no-Kami|ja|保食神|12=draft|12=draft}}, while in the Kamakura-period "Jinmyō Hisho," it is identified as {{ill|Ukanomitama|ja|ウカノミタマ|12=draft|12=draft}}<ref name="gs P155-156">Sakurai (1970): pp.155–156</ref>.
== History ==
It is believed to have existed since ancient times<ref name="gs"/>. Originally, it was part of a complex of four granaries—Tsuki-no-Mikura, Mishio-no-Mikura, Fushiki-no-Mikura, and Mishine-no-Mikura—located west of the outer fence. By the mid-Heian period, these granaries, along with the Geheiden, were moved inside the outer sacred fence<ref>Fukuyama et al. (1975): pp.228–229</ref>. At the time, the structure was built in the {{ill|azekura-zukuri|ja|校倉造|12=draft|12=draft}} style without chigi and katsuogi<ref name="ss9">Fukuyama et al. (1975): p.229</ref>. As it was within the grounds of the main sanctuary, it was relocated with each Shikinen Sengū, but all four granaries were abolished by the end of the medieval period<ref name="ss9"/>.
The rice retrieval ceremony was conducted by four officials, including {{ill|negi|ja|禰宜|12=draft|12=draft}} and Ōuchi-hito, accompanied by Ōmonoimi (great purification officials)<ref name="gs"/>. Their attire consisted of the same sacred garments used during Naikū rituals, indicating the shrine's significance<ref name="gs"/>. The retrieved rice was milled, steamed, and served in sacred vessels ({{ill|ke|ja|笥|12=draft|12=draft}}) before the deities<ref name="gs2"/>. The offerings were dedicated to Naikū, {{ill|Aramatsuri-no-Miya|ja|荒祭宮|12=draft|12=draft}}, and {{ill|Takimatsuri-no-Kami|ja|滝deity|12=draft|12=draft}}<ref name="gs2"/>.
During the {{ill|Tenshō era|ja|天正|1=Tenshō (Momoyama period)|lt=Tenshō Era|12=draft|12=draft}} (1573–1592), Mishine-no-Mikura was restored in the Yuitsu-shinmei-zukuri style northwest of the main sanctuary and has since been rebuilt with each Shikinen Sengū<ref name="ss9"/>. After the Muromachi period, it was also known as the "Sacred Weaving Hall" (御機殿) due to its association with sacred weaving during the {{ill|Kannamesai|ja|神嘗祭|12=draft|12=draft}}<ref name="ss9"/>.
Since the Meiji period, Mishine-no-Mikura-no-Kami has been formally enshrined, and in 1889, the shrine’s location was fixed along the path leading to Aramatsuri-no-Miya<ref name="ss9"/>.
== Access ==
; To Ujibashi Bridge * About 15 minutes by {{ill|Mie Kotsu|ja|三重交通|12=draft|12=draft}} bus from the south exit (JR side) of {{ill|Ise Station|ja|伊勢市駅|12=draft|12=draft}} on the {{ill|JR Sangū Line|ja|参宮線|12=draft|12=draft}} or from {{ill|Ujiyamada Station|ja|宇治山田駅|12=draft|12=draft}} on the Kintetsu Yamada and Toba Lines, alighting at the final stop "Naikū-mae"<ref name="mkr">Mie Prefecture Tourism Federation, "[http://www.kankomie.or.jp/spot/detail_2937.html Ise Jingu (Naikū) Tourist Info - Visit Mie]" (Accessed October 12, 2013)</ref>. Also accessible via bus from {{ill|Isuzugawa Station|ja|五十鈴川駅|12=draft|12=draft}} on the Kintetsu Toba Line. * About 5 minutes south via {{ill|Mie Prefectural Route 32|ja|三重県道32号伊勢磯部線|12=draft|12=draft}} (Mikimoto Road) from {{ill|Ise-nishi Interchange|ja|伊勢西インターチェンジ|12=draft|12=draft}} on the {{ill|Ise Expressway|ja|伊勢自動車道|12=draft|12=draft}}, or from {{ill|Ise Interchange|ja|伊勢インターチェンジ|12=draft|12=draft}} via {{ill|National Route 23 (Japan)|ja|国道23号|12=draft|12=draft}}. Parking available nearby<ref name="mkr"/>.
; From Ujibashi Bridge * Proceed along the path from Ujibashi toward the main sanctuary of Naikū, then take the path leading west toward Aramatsuri-no-Miya, where Mishine-no-Mikura is enshrined<ref name="ic"/>. The nearby Geheiden can also be found here<ref name="ic"/>.

'''Mishine no Mikura''' (御稲御倉, also read as *Gotō no Mikura*<ref name="ss8">Fukuyama et al. (1975): p. 228</ref>) is an auxiliary shrine under the jurisdiction of {{ill|Kōtai Jingū|ja|皇大神宮|1=Kōtai Jingū|2=ja|3=皇大神宮|4=zh|5=皇大神宮|12=draft|12=draft}} (the Inner Shrine) of {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮|12=draft|12=draft}}. It is also referred to as '''Mishine no Mikura no Kami''' (御稲御倉神)<ref name="ic">Ise Bunka-sha, ed. (2008): p. 114</ref><ref>Ujiyamada City Office, ed. (1929): p. 16</ref><ref name="gs2">Sakurai (1970): p. 156</ref>. The shrine is located within the precincts of the Inner Shrine, along the path leading to {{ill|Aramatsuri-no-Miya|ja|荒祭宮|12=draft|12=draft}}<ref name="ic"/>.
Although small, it features the same {{ill|Yuitsu Shimmei-zukuri|ja|唯一神明造|12=draft|12=draft}} architectural style as the Inner Shrine<ref name="ic"/><ref name="gk">Gakken Publishing (2013): p. 56</ref>.
== Overview ==
Located in {{ill|Ujikanda-chō|ja|宇治館町|12=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|Ise City|ja|伊勢市|12=draft|12=draft}}, Mie Prefecture, this shrine stands northwest of the main sanctuary (Shōgu) within the precincts of the {{ill|Naikū|ja|内宮|12=draft|12=draft}}<ref name="gk"/>. It Ranks 6th among the 30 shrines under the jurisdiction of Naikū. Each time a festival is held at the main sanctuary of Naikū, a ritual is also conducted at the {{ill|Mishine-no-Mikura|ja|御稲御倉|12=draft|12=draft}}<ref name="ic"/>. To the north of Mishine-no-Mikura is the {{ill|Geheiden|ja|外幣殿|12=draft|12=draft}}<ref name="ss8"/>.
The {{ill|rice|ja|イネ|12=draft|12=draft}} harvested from the sacred fields of Ise Jingu is presented as {{ill|nuiho|ja|抜穂|12=draft|12=draft}} and stored in Mishine-no-Mikura<ref name="ic"/>. This rice is later retrieved in accordance with Naikū’s rituals and offered as {{ill|Ōmikue|ja|大御饌|12=draft|12=draft}} to {{ill|Amaterasu|ja|天照大神|1=Amaterasu|lt=Amaterasu Ōmikami|12=draft|12=draft}}<ref name="ic"/><ref name="gk"/>. Essentially, it functions as the granary of Naikū<ref name="gk"/>. Following ancient customs, Shinto priests dressed in traditional garments deliver the rice to the Mikura<ref name="gs">Sakurai (1970): p.155</ref>.
The act of retrieving rice from the Mikura is called either {{ill|Gotō Hōge|ja|御稲奉下|12=draft|12=draft}} (Gotō Hōge<ref name="gs"/>, Mishine Hōge<ref name="sy">Yano (2006): p.111</ref>). It is carried out by priests who have undergone purification rites the day before<ref name="gs"/>. After retrieval, the rice undergoes threshing, dehusking, and polishing to about 70%. It is then prepared into {{ill|sacred sake|ja|神酒|12=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|mochi|ja|餅|12=draft|12=draft}}, and cooked rice ({{ill|Onii|ja|御飯|12=draft|12=draft}}) in the {{ill|Imibiya-den|ja|忌火屋殿|12=draft|12=draft}}, and offered before the deities<ref name="sy"/>.
Additionally, during the {{ill|Jingū Shikinen Sengū|ja|神宮式年遷宮|12=draft|12=draft}}, one of the key rituals involves the placement of the central sacred pillar ({{ill|Shin-no-Mihashira|ja|心御柱|12=draft|12=draft}}), which is temporarily stored in Mishine-no-Mikura<ref>{{Cite news|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20130926070226/http://sankei.jp.msn.com/life/news/130925/trd13092522350017-n1.htm|title=Shin-no-Mihashira Ceremony at Naikū for Jingū Shikinen Sengū|publisher=Sankei Shimbun|work=MSN Sankei News|date=September 25, 2013|accessdate=October 13, 2013}}</ref>.
The shrine building is constructed in the {{ill|Yuitsu-shinmei-zukuri|ja|唯一神明造|12=draft|12=draft}} style<ref name="ic"/>. It faces east toward Naikū<ref name="ic"/>. The elevated structure with a {{ill|kirizuma-style|ja|切妻|12=draft|12=draft}} roof and decorative {{ill|chigi and katsuogi|ja|千木・鰹木|12=draft|12=draft}} resembles the East and West Treasuries of Naikū and the Geheiden<ref name="ss8"/>. Due to its elevated design, a removable staircase is attached during rice retrieval. The {{ill|Gon-negi|ja|権禰宜|12=draft|12=draft}} unlocks the door with a sacred key ({{ill|Mikagi|ja|御鑰|12=draft|12=draft}}) to access the rice<ref>Yano (2006): pp.111–112</ref>. There are no surrounding {{ill|tamagaki|ja|玉垣|12=draft|12=draft}} fences or offering boxes.
== Enshrined deity ==
The enshrined deity is Mishine-no-Mikura-no-Kami (御稲御倉神)<ref name="ic"/>. This deity serves as the {{ill|guardian deity|ja|守護神|12=draft|12=draft}} of Mishine-no-Mikura<ref name="ic"/><ref name="gk"/>. During the {{ill|Jingū Shikinen Sengū|ja|神宮式年遷宮|12=draft|12=draft}}, when the shrine is rebuilt, Mishine-no-Mikura-no-Kami is temporarily transferred to the Geheiden<ref>Ise Bunka-sha (2013): p.6</ref>.
In the Heian-period text "Daiji Mikagami," the deity is referred to as {{ill|Ukemochi-no-Kami|ja|保食神|12=draft|12=draft}}, while in the Kamakura-period "Jinmyō Hisho," it is identified as {{ill|Ukanomitama|ja|ウカノミタマ|12=draft|12=draft}}<ref name="gs P155-156">Sakurai (1970): pp.155–156</ref>.
== History ==
It is believed to have existed since ancient times<ref name="gs"/>. Originally, it was part of a complex of four granaries—Tsuki-no-Mikura, Mishio-no-Mikura, Fushiki-no-Mikura, and Mishine-no-Mikura—located west of the outer fence. By the mid-Heian period, these granaries, along with the Geheiden, were moved inside the outer sacred fence<ref>Fukuyama et al. (1975): pp.228–229</ref>. At the time, the structure was built in the {{ill|azekura-zukuri|ja|校倉造|12=draft|12=draft}} style without chigi and katsuogi<ref name="ss9">Fukuyama et al. (1975): p.229</ref>. As it was within the grounds of the main sanctuary, it was relocated with each Shikinen Sengū, but all four granaries were abolished by the end of the medieval period<ref name="ss9"/>.
The rice retrieval ceremony was conducted by four officials, including {{ill|negi|ja|禰宜|12=draft|12=draft}} and Ōuchi-hito, accompanied by Ōmonoimi (great purification officials)<ref name="gs"/>. Their attire consisted of the same sacred garments used during Naikū rituals, indicating the shrine's significance<ref name="gs"/>. The retrieved rice was milled, steamed, and served in sacred vessels ({{ill|ke|ja|笥|12=draft|12=draft}}) before the deities<ref name="gs2"/>. The offerings were dedicated to Naikū, {{ill|Aramatsuri-no-Miya|ja|荒祭宮|12=draft|12=draft}}, and {{ill|Takimatsuri-no-Kami|ja|滝deity|12=draft|12=draft}}<ref name="gs2"/>.
During the {{ill|Tenshō era|ja|天正|1=Tenshō (Momoyama period)|lt=Tenshō Era|12=draft|12=draft}} (1573–1592), Mishine-no-Mikura was restored in the Yuitsu-shinmei-zukuri style northwest of the main sanctuary and has since been rebuilt with each Shikinen Sengū<ref name="ss9"/>. After the Muromachi period, it was also known as the "Sacred Weaving Hall" (御機殿) due to its association with sacred weaving during the {{ill|Kannamesai|ja|神嘗祭|12=draft|12=draft}}<ref name="ss9"/>.
Since the Meiji period, Mishine-no-Mikura-no-Kami has been formally enshrined, and in 1889, the shrine’s location was fixed along the path leading to Aramatsuri-no-Miya<ref name="ss9"/>.
== Access ==
; To Ujibashi Bridge * About 15 minutes by {{ill|Mie Kotsu|ja|三重交通|12=draft|12=draft}} bus from the south exit (JR side) of {{ill|Ise Station|ja|伊勢市駅|12=draft|12=draft}} on the {{ill|JR Sangū Line|ja|参宮線|12=draft|12=draft}} or from {{ill|Ujiyamada Station|ja|宇治山田駅|12=draft|12=draft}} on the Kintetsu Yamada and Toba Lines, alighting at the final stop "Naikū-mae"<ref name="mkr">Mie Prefecture Tourism Federation, "[http://www.kankomie.or.jp/spot/detail_2937.html Ise Jingu (Naikū) Tourist Info - Visit Mie]" (Accessed October 12, 2013)</ref>. Also accessible via bus from {{ill|Isuzugawa Station|ja|五十鈴川駅|12=draft|12=draft}} on the Kintetsu Toba Line. * About 5 minutes south via {{ill|Mie Prefectural Route 32|ja|三重県道32号伊勢磯部線|12=draft|12=draft}} (Mikimoto Road) from {{ill|Ise-nishi Interchange|ja|伊勢西インターチェンジ|12=draft|12=draft}} on the {{ill|Ise Expressway|ja|伊勢自動車道|12=draft|12=draft}}, or from {{ill|Ise Interchange|ja|伊勢インターチェンジ|12=draft|12=draft}} via {{ill|National Route 23 (Japan)|ja|国道23号|12=draft|12=draft}}. Parking available nearby<ref name="mkr"/>.
; From Ujibashi Bridge * Proceed along the path from Ujibashi toward the main sanctuary of Naikū, then take the path leading west toward Aramatsuri-no-Miya, where Mishine-no-Mikura is enshrined<ref name="ic"/>. The nearby Geheiden can also be found here<ref name="ic"/>.

{{Shrine |Name = Mishine no Mikura |religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Mishine no Mikura.jpg|270px]] |location = {{ill|Mie Prefecture|ja|三重県|12=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|Ise, Mie|ja|伊勢市|12=draft|12=draft}}, Ujikan-cho 1, within the precincts of the Inner Shrine |Latitude Degrees = 34 | Latitude Minutes = 27 | Latitude Seconds = 18.8 |Longitude Degrees = 136 | Longitude Minutes = 43 | Longitude Seconds = 28.7 |Map = Japan Mie |Label Position = bottom |deity = Mishine no Mikura no Kami |type = {{ill|Kōtai Jingū|ja|皇大神宮|1=Kōtai Jingū|2=ja|3=皇大神宮|4=zh|5=皇大神宮|12=draft|12=draft}} auxiliary shrine |Established = Ancient times |Main Hall = {{ill|Yuitsu Shimmei-zukuri|ja|唯一神明造|12=draft|12=draft}} |Other Names = |Pilgrimage Sites = |festival = |Rituals = {{ill|Kannamesai|ja|神嘗祭|12=draft|12=draft}} }}
'''Mishine no Mikura''' (御稲御倉, also read as *Gotō no Mikura*<ref name="ss8">Fukuyama et al. (1975): p. 228</ref>) is an auxiliary shrine under the jurisdiction of {{ill|Kōtai Jingū|ja|皇大神宮|1=Kōtai Jingū|2=ja|3=皇大神宮|4=zh|5=皇大神宮|12=draft|12=draft}} (the Inner Shrine) of {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮|12=draft|12=draft}}. It is also referred to as '''Mishine no Mikura no Kami''' (御稲御倉神)<ref name="ic">Ise Bunka-sha, ed. (2008): p. 114</ref><ref>Ujiyamada City Office, ed. (1929): p. 16</ref><ref name="gs2">Sakurai (1970): p. 156</ref>. The shrine is located within the precincts of the Inner Shrine, along the path leading to {{ill|Aramatsuri-no-Miya|ja|荒祭宮|12=draft|12=draft}}<ref name="ic"/>.
Although small, it features the same {{ill|Yuitsu Shimmei-zukuri|ja|唯一神明造|12=draft|12=draft}} architectural style as the Inner Shrine<ref name="ic"/><ref name="gk">Gakken Publishing (2013): p. 56</ref>.
== Overview ==
Located in {{ill|Ujikanda-chō|ja|宇治館町|12=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|Ise City|ja|伊勢市|12=draft|12=draft}}, Mie Prefecture, this shrine stands northwest of the main sanctuary (Shōgu) within the precincts of the {{ill|Naikū|ja|内宮|12=draft|12=draft}}<ref name="gk"/>. It Ranks 6th among the 30 shrines under the jurisdiction of Naikū. Each time a festival is held at the main sanctuary of Naikū, a ritual is also conducted at the {{ill|Mishine-no-Mikura|ja|御稲御倉|12=draft|12=draft}}<ref name="ic"/>. To the north of Mishine-no-Mikura is the {{ill|Geheiden|ja|外幣殿|12=draft|12=draft}}<ref name="ss8"/>.
The {{ill|rice|ja|イネ|12=draft|12=draft}} harvested from the sacred fields of Ise Jingu is presented as {{ill|nuiho|ja|抜穂|12=draft|12=draft}} and stored in Mishine-no-Mikura<ref name="ic"/>. This rice is later retrieved in accordance with Naikū’s rituals and offered as {{ill|Ōmikue|ja|大御饌|12=draft|12=draft}} to {{ill|Amaterasu|ja|天照大神|1=Amaterasu|lt=Amaterasu Ōmikami|12=draft|12=draft}}<ref name="ic"/><ref name="gk"/>. Essentially, it functions as the granary of Naikū<ref name="gk"/>. Following ancient customs, Shinto priests dressed in traditional garments deliver the rice to the Mikura<ref name="gs">Sakurai (1970): p.155</ref>.
The act of retrieving rice from the Mikura is called either {{ill|Gotō Hōge|ja|御稲奉下|12=draft|12=draft}} (Gotō Hōge<ref name="gs"/>, Mishine Hōge<ref name="sy">Yano (2006): p.111</ref>). It is carried out by priests who have undergone purification rites the day before<ref name="gs"/>. After retrieval, the rice undergoes threshing, dehusking, and polishing to about 70%. It is then prepared into {{ill|sacred sake|ja|神酒|12=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|mochi|ja|餅|12=draft|12=draft}}, and cooked rice ({{ill|Onii|ja|御飯|12=draft|12=draft}}) in the {{ill|Imibiya-den|ja|忌火屋殿|12=draft|12=draft}}, and offered before the deities<ref name="sy"/>.
Additionally, during the {{ill|Jingū Shikinen Sengū|ja|神宮式年遷宮|12=draft|12=draft}}, one of the key rituals involves the placement of the central sacred pillar ({{ill|Shin-no-Mihashira|ja|心御柱|12=draft|12=draft}}), which is temporarily stored in Mishine-no-Mikura<ref>{{Cite news|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20130926070226/http://sankei.jp.msn.com/life/news/130925/trd13092522350017-n1.htm|title=Shin-no-Mihashira Ceremony at Naikū for Jingū Shikinen Sengū|publisher=Sankei Shimbun|work=MSN Sankei News|date=September 25, 2013|accessdate=October 13, 2013}}</ref>.
The shrine building is constructed in the {{ill|Yuitsu-shinmei-zukuri|ja|唯一神明造|12=draft|12=draft}} style<ref name="ic"/>. It faces east toward Naikū<ref name="ic"/>. The elevated structure with a {{ill|kirizuma-style|ja|切妻|12=draft|12=draft}} roof and decorative {{ill|chigi and katsuogi|ja|千木・鰹木|12=draft|12=draft}} resembles the East and West Treasuries of Naikū and the Geheiden<ref name="ss8"/>. Due to its elevated design, a removable staircase is attached during rice retrieval. The {{ill|Gon-negi|ja|権禰宜|12=draft|12=draft}} unlocks the door with a sacred key ({{ill|Mikagi|ja|御鑰|12=draft|12=draft}}) to access the rice<ref>Yano (2006): pp.111–112</ref>. There are no surrounding {{ill|tamagaki|ja|玉垣|12=draft|12=draft}} fences or offering boxes.
== Enshrined deity ==
The enshrined deity is Mishine-no-Mikura-no-Kami (御稲御倉神)<ref name="ic"/>. This deity serves as the {{ill|guardian deity|ja|守護神|12=draft|12=draft}} of Mishine-no-Mikura<ref name="ic"/><ref name="gk"/>. During the {{ill|Jingū Shikinen Sengū|ja|神宮式年遷宮|12=draft|12=draft}}, when the shrine is rebuilt, Mishine-no-Mikura-no-Kami is temporarily transferred to the Geheiden<ref>Ise Bunka-sha (2013): p.6</ref>.
In the Heian-period text "Daiji Mikagami," the deity is referred to as {{ill|Ukemochi-no-Kami|ja|保食神|12=draft|12=draft}}, while in the Kamakura-period "Jinmyō Hisho," it is identified as {{ill|Ukanomitama|ja|ウカノミタマ|12=draft|12=draft}}<ref name="gs P155-156">Sakurai (1970): pp.155–156</ref>.
== History ==
It is believed to have existed since ancient times<ref name="gs"/>. Originally, it was part of a complex of four granaries—Tsuki-no-Mikura, Mishio-no-Mikura, Fushiki-no-Mikura, and Mishine-no-Mikura—located west of the outer fence. By the mid-Heian period, these granaries, along with the Geheiden, were moved inside the outer sacred fence<ref>Fukuyama et al. (1975): pp.228–229</ref>. At the time, the structure was built in the {{ill|azekura-zukuri|ja|校倉造|12=draft|12=draft}} style without chigi and katsuogi<ref name="ss9">Fukuyama et al. (1975): p.229</ref>. As it was within the grounds of the main sanctuary, it was relocated with each Shikinen Sengū, but all four granaries were abolished by the end of the medieval period<ref name="ss9"/>.
The rice retrieval ceremony was conducted by four officials, including {{ill|negi|ja|禰宜|12=draft|12=draft}} and Ōuchi-hito, accompanied by Ōmonoimi (great purification officials)<ref name="gs"/>. Their attire consisted of the same sacred garments used during Naikū rituals, indicating the shrine's significance<ref name="gs"/>. The retrieved rice was milled, steamed, and served in sacred vessels ({{ill|ke|ja|笥|12=draft|12=draft}}) before the deities<ref name="gs2"/>. The offerings were dedicated to Naikū, {{ill|Aramatsuri-no-Miya|ja|荒祭宮|12=draft|12=draft}}, and {{ill|Takimatsuri-no-Kami|ja|滝deity|12=draft|12=draft}}<ref name="gs2"/>.
During the {{ill|Tenshō era|ja|天正|1=Tenshō (Momoyama period)|lt=Tenshō Era|12=draft|12=draft}} (1573–1592), Mishine-no-Mikura was restored in the Yuitsu-shinmei-zukuri style northwest of the main sanctuary and has since been rebuilt with each Shikinen Sengū<ref name="ss9"/>. After the Muromachi period, it was also known as the "Sacred Weaving Hall" (御機殿) due to its association with sacred weaving during the {{ill|Kannamesai|ja|神嘗祭|12=draft|12=draft}}<ref name="ss9"/>.
Since the Meiji period, Mishine-no-Mikura-no-Kami has been formally enshrined, and in 1889, the shrine’s location was fixed along the path leading to Aramatsuri-no-Miya<ref name="ss9"/>.
== Access ==
; To Ujibashi Bridge * About 15 minutes by {{ill|Mie Kotsu|ja|三重交通|12=draft|12=draft}} bus from the south exit (JR side) of {{ill|Ise Station|ja|伊勢市駅|12=draft|12=draft}} on the {{ill|JR Sangū Line|ja|参宮線|12=draft|12=draft}} or from {{ill|Ujiyamada Station|ja|Ujiyamada Station|12=draft|12=draft}} on the Kintetsu Yamada and Toba Lines, alighting at the final stop "Naikū-mae"<ref name="mkr">Mie Prefecture Tourism Federation, "[http://www.kankomie.or.jp/spot/detail_2937.html Ise Jingu (Naikū) Tourist Info - Visit Mie]" (Accessed October 12, 2013)</ref>. Also accessible via bus from {{ill|Isuzugawa Station|ja|五十鈴川駅|12=draft|12=draft}} on the Kintetsu Toba Line. * About 5 minutes south via {{ill|Mie Prefectural Route 32|ja|三重県道32号伊勢磯部線|12=draft|12=draft}} (Mikimoto Road) from {{ill|Ise-nishi Interchange|ja|伊勢西インターチェンジ|12=draft|12=draft}} on the {{ill|Ise Expressway|ja|伊勢自動車道|12=draft|12=draft}}, or from {{ill|Ise Interchange|ja|伊勢インターチェンジ|12=draft|12=draft}} via {{ill|National Route 23 (Japan)|ja|国道23号|12=draft|12=draft}}. Parking available nearby<ref name="mkr"/>.
; From Ujibashi Bridge * Proceed along the path from Ujibashi toward the main sanctuary of Naikū, then take the path leading west toward Aramatsuri-no-Miya, where Mishine-no-Mikura is enshrined<ref name="ic"/>. The nearby Geheiden can also be found here<ref name="ic"/>.

<!--Mishine-no-Mikura-->

revision.comment: Text replacement - "伊勢市の神社" to "Shrines in Ise City"
revision.text:
{{Shrine |Name = Mishine no Mikura |religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Mishine no Mikura.jpg|270px]] |location = {{ill|Mie Prefecture|ja|三重県|12=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|Ise, Mie|ja|伊勢市|12=draft|12=draft}}, Ujikan-cho 1, within the precincts of the Inner Shrine |Latitude Degrees = 34 | Latitude Minutes = 27 | Latitude Seconds = 18.8 |Longitude Degrees = 136 | Longitude Minutes = 43 | Longitude Seconds = 28.7 |Map = Japan Mie |Label Position = bottom |deity = Mishine no Mikura no Kami |type = {{ill|Kōtai Jingū|ja|皇大神宮|1=Kōtai Jingū|2=ja|3=皇大神宮|4=zh|5=皇大神宮|12=draft|12=draft}} auxiliary shrine |Established = Ancient times |Main Hall = {{ill|Yuitsu Shimmei-zukuri|ja|唯一神明造|12=draft|12=draft}} |Other Names = |Pilgrimage Sites = |festival = |Rituals = {{ill|Kannamesai|ja|神嘗祭|12=draft|12=draft}} }}
'''Mishine no Mikura''' (御稲御倉, also read as *Gotō no Mikura*<ref name="ss8">Fukuyama et al. (1975): p. 228</ref>) is an auxiliary shrine under the jurisdiction of {{ill|Kōtai Jingū|ja|皇大神宮|1=Kōtai Jingū|2=ja|3=皇大神宮|4=zh|5=皇大神宮|12=draft|12=draft}} (the Inner Shrine) of {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮|12=draft|12=draft}}. It is also referred to as '''Mishine no Mikura no Kami''' (御稲御倉神)<ref name="ic">Ise Bunka-sha, ed. (2008): p. 114</ref><ref>Ujiyamada City Office, ed. (1929): p. 16</ref><ref name="gs2">Sakurai (1970): p. 156</ref>. The shrine is located within the precincts of the Inner Shrine, along the path leading to {{ill|Aramatsuri-no-Miya|ja|荒祭宮|12=draft|12=draft}}<ref name="ic"/>.
Although small, it features the same {{ill|Yuitsu Shimmei-zukuri|ja|唯一神明造|12=draft|12=draft}} architectural style as the Inner Shrine<ref name="ic"/><ref name="gk">Gakken Publishing (2013): p. 56</ref>.
== Overview ==
Located in {{ill|Ujikanda-chō|ja|宇治館町|12=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|Ise City|ja|伊勢市|12=draft|12=draft}}, Mie Prefecture, this shrine stands northwest of the main sanctuary (Shōgu) within the precincts of the {{ill|Naikū|ja|内宮|12=draft|12=draft}}<ref name="gk"/>. It Ranks 6th among the 30 shrines under the jurisdiction of Naikū. Each time a festival is held at the main sanctuary of Naikū, a ritual is also conducted at the {{ill|Mishine-no-Mikura|ja|御稲御倉|12=draft|12=draft}}<ref name="ic"/>. To the north of Mishine-no-Mikura is the {{ill|Geheiden|ja|外幣殿|12=draft|12=draft}}<ref name="ss8"/>.
The {{ill|rice|ja|イネ|12=draft|12=draft}} harvested from the sacred fields of Ise Jingu is presented as {{ill|nuiho|ja|抜穂|12=draft|12=draft}} and stored in Mishine-no-Mikura<ref name="ic"/>. This rice is later retrieved in accordance with Naikū’s rituals and offered as {{ill|Ōmikue|ja|大御饌|12=draft|12=draft}} to {{ill|Amaterasu|ja|天照大神|1=Amaterasu|lt=Amaterasu Ōmikami|12=draft|12=draft}}<ref name="ic"/><ref name="gk"/>. Essentially, it functions as the granary of Naikū<ref name="gk"/>. Following ancient customs, Shinto priests dressed in traditional garments deliver the rice to the Mikura<ref name="gs">Sakurai (1970): p.155</ref>.
The act of retrieving rice from the Mikura is called either {{ill|Gotō Hōge|ja|御稲奉下|12=draft|12=draft}} (Gotō Hōge<ref name="gs"/>, Mishine Hōge<ref name="sy">Yano (2006): p.111</ref>). It is carried out by priests who have undergone purification rites the day before<ref name="gs"/>. After retrieval, the rice undergoes threshing, dehusking, and polishing to about 70%. It is then prepared into {{ill|sacred sake|ja|神酒|12=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|mochi|ja|餅|12=draft|12=draft}}, and cooked rice ({{ill|Onii|ja|御飯|12=draft|12=draft}}) in the {{ill|Imibiya-den|ja|忌火屋殿|12=draft|12=draft}}, and offered before the deities<ref name="sy"/>.
Additionally, during the {{ill|Jingū Shikinen Sengū|ja|神宮式年遷宮|12=draft|12=draft}}, one of the key rituals involves the placement of the central sacred pillar ({{ill|Shin-no-Mihashira|ja|心御柱|12=draft|12=draft}}), which is temporarily stored in Mishine-no-Mikura<ref>{{Cite news|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20130926070226/http://sankei.jp.msn.com/life/news/130925/trd13092522350017-n1.htm|title=Shin-no-Mihashira Ceremony at Naikū for Jingū Shikinen Sengū|publisher=Sankei Shimbun|work=MSN Sankei News|date=September 25, 2013|accessdate=October 13, 2013}}</ref>.
The shrine building is constructed in the {{ill|Yuitsu-shinmei-zukuri|ja|唯一神明造|12=draft|12=draft}} style<ref name="ic"/>. It faces east toward Naikū<ref name="ic"/>. The elevated structure with a {{ill|kirizuma-style|ja|切妻|12=draft|12=draft}} roof and decorative {{ill|chigi and katsuogi|ja|千木・鰹木|12=draft|12=draft}} resembles the East and West Treasuries of Naikū and the Geheiden<ref name="ss8"/>. Due to its elevated design, a removable staircase is attached during rice retrieval. The {{ill|Gon-negi|ja|権禰宜|12=draft|12=draft}} unlocks the door with a sacred key ({{ill|Mikagi|ja|御鑰|12=draft|12=draft}}) to access the rice<ref>Yano (2006): pp.111–112</ref>. There are no surrounding {{ill|tamagaki|ja|玉垣|12=draft|12=draft}} fences or offering boxes.
== Enshrined deity ==
The enshrined deity is Mishine-no-Mikura-no-Kami (御稲御倉神)<ref name="ic"/>. This deity serves as the {{ill|guardian deity|ja|守護神|12=draft|12=draft}} of Mishine-no-Mikura<ref name="ic"/><ref name="gk"/>. During the {{ill|Jingū Shikinen Sengū|ja|神宮式年遷宮|12=draft|12=draft}}, when the shrine is rebuilt, Mishine-no-Mikura-no-Kami is temporarily transferred to the Geheiden<ref>Ise Bunka-sha (2013): p.6</ref>.
In the Heian-period text "Daiji Mikagami," the deity is referred to as {{ill|Ukemochi-no-Kami|ja|保食神|12=draft|12=draft}}, while in the Kamakura-period "Jinmyō Hisho," it is identified as {{ill|Ukanomitama|ja|ウカノミタマ|12=draft|12=draft}}<ref name="gs P155-156">Sakurai (1970): pp.155–156</ref>.
== History ==
It is believed to have existed since ancient times<ref name="gs"/>. Originally, it was part of a complex of four granaries—Tsuki-no-Mikura, Mishio-no-Mikura, Fushiki-no-Mikura, and Mishine-no-Mikura—located west of the outer fence. By the mid-Heian period, these granaries, along with the Geheiden, were moved inside the outer sacred fence<ref>Fukuyama et al. (1975): pp.228–229</ref>. At the time, the structure was built in the {{ill|azekura-zukuri|ja|校倉造|12=draft|12=draft}} style without chigi and katsuogi<ref name="ss9">Fukuyama et al. (1975): p.229</ref>. As it was within the grounds of the main sanctuary, it was relocated with each Shikinen Sengū, but all four granaries were abolished by the end of the medieval period<ref name="ss9"/>.
The rice retrieval ceremony was conducted by four officials, including {{ill|negi|ja|禰宜|12=draft|12=draft}} and Ōuchi-hito, accompanied by Ōmonoimi (great purification officials)<ref name="gs"/>. Their attire consisted of the same sacred garments used during Naikū rituals, indicating the shrine's significance<ref name="gs"/>. The retrieved rice was milled, steamed, and served in sacred vessels ({{ill|ke|ja|笥|12=draft|12=draft}}) before the deities<ref name="gs2"/>. The offerings were dedicated to Naikū, {{ill|Aramatsuri-no-Miya|ja|荒祭宮|12=draft|12=draft}}, and {{ill|Takimatsuri-no-Kami|ja|滝deity|12=draft|12=draft}}<ref name="gs2"/>.
During the {{ill|Tenshō era|ja|天正|1=Tenshō (Momoyama period)|lt=Tenshō Era|12=draft|12=draft}} (1573–1592), Mishine-no-Mikura was restored in the Yuitsu-shinmei-zukuri style northwest of the main sanctuary and has since been rebuilt with each Shikinen Sengū<ref name="ss9"/>. After the Muromachi period, it was also known as the "Sacred Weaving Hall" (御機殿) due to its association with sacred weaving during the {{ill|Kannamesai|ja|神嘗祭|12=draft|12=draft}}<ref name="ss9"/>.
Since the Meiji period, Mishine-no-Mikura-no-Kami has been formally enshrined, and in 1889, the shrine’s location was fixed along the path leading to Aramatsuri-no-Miya<ref name="ss9"/>.
== Access ==
; To Ujibashi Bridge * About 15 minutes by {{ill|Mie Kotsu|ja|三重交通|12=draft|12=draft}} bus from the south exit (JR side) of {{ill|Ise Station|ja|伊勢市駅|12=draft|12=draft}} on the {{ill|JR Sangū Line|ja|参宮線|12=draft|12=draft}} or from {{ill|Ujiyamada Station|ja|Ujiyamada Station|12=draft|12=draft}} on the Kintetsu Yamada and Toba Lines, alighting at the final stop "Naikū-mae"<ref name="mkr">Mie Prefecture Tourism Federation, "[http://www.kankomie.or.jp/spot/detail_2937.html Ise Jingu (Naikū) Tourist Info - Visit Mie]" (Accessed October 12, 2013)</ref>. Also accessible via bus from {{ill|Isuzugawa Station|ja|五十鈴川駅|12=draft|12=draft}} on the Kintetsu Toba Line. * About 5 minutes south via {{ill|Mie Prefectural Route 32|ja|三重県道32号伊勢磯部線|12=draft|12=draft}} (Mikimoto Road) from {{ill|Ise-nishi Interchange|ja|伊勢西インターチェンジ|12=draft|12=draft}} on the {{ill|Ise Expressway|ja|伊勢自動車道|12=draft|12=draft}}, or from {{ill|Ise Interchange|ja|伊勢インターチェンジ|12=draft|12=draft}} via {{ill|National Route 23 (Japan)|ja|国道23号|12=draft|12=draft}}. Parking available nearby<ref name="mkr"/>.
; From Ujibashi Bridge * Proceed along the path from Ujibashi toward the main sanctuary of Naikū, then take the path leading west toward Aramatsuri-no-Miya, where Mishine-no-Mikura is enshrined<ref name="ic"/>. The nearby Geheiden can also be found here<ref name="ic"/>.

Since the Meiji period, Mishine-no-Mikura-no-Kami has been formally enshrined, and in 1889, the shrine’s location was fixed along the path leading to Aramatsuri-no-Miya<ref name="ss9"/>.
== Access ==
; To Ujibashi Bridge * About 15 minutes by {{ill|Mie Kotsu|ja|三重交通}} bus from the south exit (JR side) of {{ill|Ise Station|ja|伊勢市駅}} on the {{ill|JR Sangū Line|ja|参宮線}} or from {{ill|Ujiyamada Station|ja|Ujiyamada Station}} on the Kintetsu Yamada and Toba Lines, alighting at the final stop "Naikū-mae"<ref name="mkr">Mie Prefecture Tourism Federation, "[http://www.kankomie.or.jp/spot/detail_2937.html Ise Jingu (Naikū) Tourist Info - Visit Mie]" (Accessed October 12, 2013)</ref>. Also accessible via bus from {{ill|Isuzugawa Station|ja|五十鈴川駅}} on the Kintetsu Toba Line. * About 5 minutes south via {{ill|Mie Prefectural Route 32|ja|三重県道32号伊勢磯部線}} (Mikimoto Road) from {{ill|Ise-nishi Interchange|ja|伊勢西インターチェンジ}} on the {{ill|Ise Expressway|ja|伊勢自動車道}}, or from {{ill|Ise Interchange|ja|伊勢インターチェンジ}} via {{ill|National Route 23 (Japan)|ja|国道23号}}. Parking available nearby<ref name="mkr"/>.
; From Ujibashi Bridge * Proceed along the path from Ujibashi toward the main sanctuary of Naikū, then take the path leading west toward Aramatsuri-no-Miya, where Mishine-no-Mikura is enshrined<ref name="ic"/>. The nearby Geheiden can also be found here<ref name="ic"/>.

revision.comment: Text replacement - "|保食神" to "|保食神|1=Ukemochi"
revision.text:
{{Shrine |Name = Mishine no Mikura |religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Mishine no Mikura.jpg|270px]] |location = {{ill|Mie Prefecture|ja|三重県}}, {{ill|Ise, Mie|ja|伊勢市}}, Ujikan-cho 1, within the precincts of the Inner Shrine |Latitude Degrees = 34 | Latitude Minutes = 27 | Latitude Seconds = 18.8 |Longitude Degrees = 136 | Longitude Minutes = 43 | Longitude Seconds = 28.7 |Map = Japan Mie |Label Position = bottom |deity = Mishine no Mikura no Kami |type = {{ill|Kōtai Jingū|ja|皇大神宮|1=Kōtai Jingū|2=ja|3=皇大神宮|4=zh|5=皇大神宮}} auxiliary shrine |Established = Ancient times |Main Hall = {{ill|Yuitsu Shimmei-zukuri|ja|唯一神明造}} |Other Names = |Pilgrimage Sites = |festival = |Rituals = {{ill|Kannamesai|ja|神嘗祭}} }}
'''Mishine no Mikura''' (御稲御倉, also read as *Gotō no Mikura*<ref name="ss8">Fukuyama et al. (1975): p. 228</ref>) is an auxiliary shrine under the jurisdiction of {{ill|Kōtai Jingū|ja|皇大神宮|1=Kōtai Jingū|2=ja|3=皇大神宮|4=zh|5=皇大神宮}} (the Inner Shrine) of {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮}}. It is also referred to as '''Mishine no Mikura no Kami''' (御稲御倉神)<ref name="ic">Ise Bunka-sha, ed. (2008): p. 114</ref><ref>Ujiyamada City Office, ed. (1929): p. 16</ref><ref name="gs2">Sakurai (1970): p. 156</ref>. The shrine is located within the precincts of the Inner Shrine, along the path leading to {{ill|Aramatsuri-no-Miya|ja|荒祭宮}}<ref name="ic"/>.
Although small, it features the same {{ill|Yuitsu Shimmei-zukuri|ja|唯一神明造}} architectural style as the Inner Shrine<ref name="ic"/><ref name="gk">Gakken Publishing (2013): p. 56</ref>.
== Overview ==
Located in {{ill|Ujikanda-chō|ja|宇治館町}}, {{ill|Ise City|ja|伊勢市}}, Mie Prefecture, this shrine stands northwest of the main sanctuary (Shōgu) within the precincts of the {{ill|Naikū|ja|内宮}}<ref name="gk"/>. It Ranks 6th among the 30 shrines under the jurisdiction of Naikū. Each time a festival is held at the main sanctuary of Naikū, a ritual is also conducted at the {{ill|Mishine-no-Mikura|ja|御稲御倉}}<ref name="ic"/>. To the north of Mishine-no-Mikura is the {{ill|Geheiden|ja|外幣殿}}<ref name="ss8"/>.
The {{ill|rice|ja|イネ}} harvested from the sacred fields of Ise Jingu is presented as {{ill|nuiho|ja|抜穂}} and stored in Mishine-no-Mikura<ref name="ic"/>. This rice is later retrieved in accordance with Naikū’s rituals and offered as {{ill|Ōmikue|ja|大御饌}} to {{ill|Amaterasu|ja|天照大神|1=Amaterasu|lt=Amaterasu Ōmikami}}<ref name="ic"/><ref name="gk"/>. Essentially, it functions as the granary of Naikū<ref name="gk"/>. Following ancient customs, Shinto priests dressed in traditional garments deliver the rice to the Mikura<ref name="gs">Sakurai (1970): p.155</ref>.
The act of retrieving rice from the Mikura is called either {{ill|Gotō Hōge|ja|御稲奉下}} (Gotō Hōge<ref name="gs"/>, Mishine Hōge<ref name="sy">Yano (2006): p.111</ref>). It is carried out by priests who have undergone purification rites the day before<ref name="gs"/>. After retrieval, the rice undergoes threshing, dehusking, and polishing to about 70%. It is then prepared into {{ill|sacred sake|ja|神酒}}, {{ill|mochi|ja|餅}}, and cooked rice ({{ill|Onii|ja|御飯}}) in the {{ill|Imibiya-den|ja|忌火屋殿}}, and offered before the deities<ref name="sy"/>.
Additionally, during the {{ill|Jingū Shikinen Sengū|ja|神宮式年遷宮}}, one of the key rituals involves the placement of the central sacred pillar ({{ill|Shin-no-Mihashira|ja|心御柱}}), which is temporarily stored in Mishine-no-Mikura<ref>{{Cite news|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20130926070226/http://sankei.jp.msn.com/life/news/130925/trd13092522350017-n1.htm|title=Shin-no-Mihashira Ceremony at Naikū for Jingū Shikinen Sengū|publisher=Sankei Shimbun|work=MSN Sankei News|date=September 25, 2013|accessdate=October 13, 2013}}</ref>.
The shrine building is constructed in the {{ill|Yuitsu-shinmei-zukuri|ja|唯一神明造}} style<ref name="ic"/>. It faces east toward Naikū<ref name="ic"/>. The elevated structure with a {{ill|kirizuma-style|ja|切妻}} roof and decorative {{ill|chigi and katsuogi|ja|千木・鰹木}} resembles the East and West Treasuries of Naikū and the Geheiden<ref name="ss8"/>. Due to its elevated design, a removable staircase is attached during rice retrieval. The {{ill|Gon-negi|ja|権禰宜}} unlocks the door with a sacred key ({{ill|Mikagi|ja|御鑰}}) to access the rice<ref>Yano (2006): pp.111–112</ref>. There are no surrounding {{ill|tamagaki|ja|玉垣}} fences or offering boxes.
== Enshrined deity ==
The enshrined deity is Mishine-no-Mikura-no-Kami (御稲御倉神)<ref name="ic"/>. This deity serves as the {{ill|guardian deity|ja|守護神}} of Mishine-no-Mikura<ref name="ic"/><ref name="gk"/>. During the {{ill|Jingū Shikinen Sengū|ja|神宮式年遷宮}}, when the shrine is rebuilt, Mishine-no-Mikura-no-Kami is temporarily transferred to the Geheiden<ref>Ise Bunka-sha (2013): p.6</ref>.
In the Heian-period text "Daiji Mikagami," the deity is referred to as {{ill|Ukemochi-no-Kami|ja|保食神|1=Ukemochi}}, while in the Kamakura-period "Jinmyō Hisho," it is identified as {{ill|Ukanomitama|ja|ウカノミタマ}}<ref name="gs P155-156">Sakurai (1970): pp.155–156</ref>.
== History ==
It is believed to have existed since ancient times<ref name="gs"/>. Originally, it was part of a complex of four granaries—Tsuki-no-Mikura, Mishio-no-Mikura, Fushiki-no-Mikura, and Mishine-no-Mikura—located west of the outer fence. By the mid-Heian period, these granaries, along with the Geheiden, were moved inside the outer sacred fence<ref>Fukuyama et al. (1975): pp.228–229</ref>. At the time, the structure was built in the {{ill|azekura-zukuri|ja|校倉造}} style without chigi and katsuogi<ref name="ss9">Fukuyama et al. (1975): p.229</ref>. As it was within the grounds of the main sanctuary, it was relocated with each Shikinen Sengū, but all four granaries were abolished by the end of the medieval period<ref name="ss9"/>.
The rice retrieval ceremony was conducted by four officials, including {{ill|negi|ja|禰宜}} and Ōuchi-hito, accompanied by Ōmonoimi (great purification officials)<ref name="gs"/>. Their attire consisted of the same sacred garments used during Naikū rituals, indicating the shrine's significance<ref name="gs"/>. The retrieved rice was milled, steamed, and served in sacred vessels ({{ill|ke|ja|笥}}) before the deities<ref name="gs2"/>. The offerings were dedicated to Naikū, {{ill|Aramatsuri-no-Miya|ja|荒祭宮}}, and {{ill|Takimatsuri-no-Kami|ja|滝deity}}<ref name="gs2"/>.
During the {{ill|Tenshō era|ja|天正|1=Tenshō (Momoyama period)|lt=Tenshō Era}} (1573–1592), Mishine-no-Mikura was restored in the Yuitsu-shinmei-zukuri style northwest of the main sanctuary and has since been rebuilt with each Shikinen Sengū<ref name="ss9"/>. After the Muromachi period, it was also known as the "Sacred Weaving Hall" (御機殿) due to its association with sacred weaving during the {{ill|Kannamesai|ja|神嘗祭}}<ref name="ss9"/>.
Since the Meiji period, Mishine-no-Mikura-no-Kami has been formally enshrined, and in 1889, the shrine’s location was fixed along the path leading to Aramatsuri-no-Miya<ref name="ss9"/>.
== Access ==
; To Ujibashi Bridge * About 15 minutes by {{ill|Mie Kotsu|ja|三重交通}} bus from the south exit (JR side) of {{ill|Ise Station|ja|伊勢市駅}} on the {{ill|JR Sangū Line|ja|参宮線}} or from {{ill|Ujiyamada Station|ja|Ujiyamada Station}} on the Kintetsu Yamada and Toba Lines, alighting at the final stop "Naikū-mae"<ref name="mkr">Mie Prefecture Tourism Federation, "[http://www.kankomie.or.jp/spot/detail_2937.html Ise Jingu (Naikū) Tourist Info - Visit Mie]" (Accessed October 12, 2013)</ref>. Also accessible via bus from {{ill|Isuzugawa Station|ja|五十鈴川駅}} on the Kintetsu Toba Line. * About 5 minutes south via {{ill|Mie Prefectural Route 32|ja|三重県道32号伊勢磯部線}} (Mikimoto Road) from {{ill|Ise-nishi Interchange|ja|伊勢西インターチェンジ}} on the {{ill|Ise Expressway|ja|伊勢自動車道}}, or from {{ill|Ise Interchange|ja|伊勢インターチェンジ}} via {{ill|National Route 23 (Japan)|ja|国道23号}}. Parking available nearby<ref name="mkr"/>.
; From Ujibashi Bridge * Proceed along the path from Ujibashi toward the main sanctuary of Naikū, then take the path leading west toward Aramatsuri-no-Miya, where Mishine-no-Mikura is enshrined<ref name="ic"/>. The nearby Geheiden can also be found here<ref name="ic"/>.

revision.comment: 16 revisions imported: Bot: import full history from ja:御稲御倉 up to rev 100409105
revision.text:
{{神社 |名称= 御稲御倉 |画像= [[ファイル:Mishine no Mikura.jpg|270px]] |所在地 = [[三重県]][[伊勢市]]宇治館町1 内宮宮域内 |緯度度 = 34 | 緯度分 = 27 | 緯度秒 = 18.8 |経度度 = 136 |経度分 = 43 | 経度秒 = 28.7 |地図= Japan Mie |ラベル位置=bottom |祭神= 御稲御倉神 |社格= [[皇大神宮]]所管社 |創建= [[古代]] |本殿= [[唯一神明造]] |別名= |札所等= |例祭= |神事= [[神嘗祭]] }}
'''御稲御倉'''（みしねのみくら、ごとうのみくら<ref name="ss8">福山ほか（1975）：228ページ</ref>）は、[[伊勢神宮]][[皇大神宮]]（内宮）の所管社。'''御稲御倉神'''（みしねのみくらのかみ）とも称する<ref name="ic">伊勢文化舎 編（2008）：114ページ</ref><ref>宇治山田市役所 編（1929）：16ページ</ref><ref name="gs2">櫻井（1970）：156ページ</ref>。内宮の宮域内、[[荒祭宮]]に向かう道の途中に鎮座する<ref name="ic"/>。
小さいながら、内宮と同じ[[唯一神明造]]の[[神社]]である<ref name="ic"/><ref name="gk">学研パブリッシング（2013）：56ページ</ref>。
== 概要 == [[三重県]][[伊勢市]]宇治館町、内宮の宮域、正宮の北西に鎮座する<ref name="gk"/>。内宮の所管社30社のうち第6位である。内宮の正宮で[[祭]]が行われるたびに御稲御倉でも[[祭祀]]が行われる<ref name="ic"/>。御稲御倉の北には外幣殿（げへいでん）がある<ref name="ss8"/>。
神宮神田で収穫された[[イネ]]は抜穂（ぬいぼ）にして、御稲御倉へ納められる<ref name="ic"/>。御倉のイネは内宮の祭祀に合わせて取り出され、[[神饌|大御饌]]として[[アマテラス|天照大神]]に捧げられる<ref name="ic"/><ref name="gk"/>。すなわち内宮[[穀倉]]として利用されている<ref name="gk"/>。古式に則り、[[装束]]をまとった[[神職]]が御倉へ納める<ref name="gs">櫻井（1970）：155ページ</ref>。御倉からイネを取り出すことを御稲奉下（ごとうほうげ<ref name="gs"/>、みしねほうげ<ref name="sy">矢野（2006）：111ページ</ref>）と称し、前日から参籠潔斎（さんろうけっさい）した神職が行う<ref name="gs"/>。奉下されたイネは稲扱（いねこき）、籾摺（もみすり）、七分搗きにした後、忌火屋殿で[[神酒]]・[[餅|御餅]]・御飯（おんいい）に調理され、神前に供される<ref name="sy"/>。
なお、[[神宮式年遷宮]]の祭事の1つで、正殿の床下に柱を立てる「心御柱奉建」（しんのみはしらほうけん）で用いる心御柱も御稲御倉に納められる<ref>{{Cite news|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20130926070226/http://sankei.jp.msn.com/life/news/130925/trd13092522350017-n1.htm|title=内宮で心御柱奉建 伊勢神宮式年遷宮|publisher=[[産経新聞]]|work=MSN産経ニュース|date=2013年9月25日|accessdate=2013年10月13日|archiveurl=https://megalodon.jp/2013-1013-0152-17/sankei.jp.msn.com/life/news/130925/trd13092522350017-n1.htm|archivedate=2013年10月13日}}</ref>。
[[社殿]]は唯一神明造である<ref name="ic"/>。内宮の方角（東）を向いて建っている<ref name="ic"/>。[[高床建物|高床]]の[[切妻]]屋根で、[[千木・鰹木]]を備えるなど、内宮の東西宝殿や外幣殿に酷似した構造である<ref name="ss8"/>。高床であるため、御稲奉下の際には取り外し可能な階段を取り付け、[[権禰宜]]が御鑰（みかぎ）を開錠して御扉を開き、イネを取り出す<ref>矢野（2006）：111 - 112ページ</ref>。[[玉垣]]や[[賽銭]]箱はない。
== 祭神 == [[祭神]]は御稲御倉神（みしねのみくらのかみ）<ref name="ic"/>。御稲御倉の[[守護神]]である<ref name="ic"/><ref name="gk"/>。[[神宮式年遷宮]]に伴って社殿が建て替えられる際には、御稲御倉神は一時的に外幣殿に遷座する<ref>伊勢文化舎 編（2013）：6ページ</ref>。
[[平安時代]]の『大治御形記』には[[保食神]]（うけもちのかみ）とあり、[[鎌倉時代]]の『神名秘書』では[[ウカノミタマ]]とする<ref name="gs P155-156">櫻井（1970）：155 - 156ページ</ref>。
== 歴史 == [[古代]]より存在したと考えられる<ref name="gs"/>。調御倉（つきのみくら）・御塩御倉・鋪設御倉・御稲御倉・御稲御倉の4棟で1組として板垣外の西方に建っていたが、[[平安時代]]中頃より、外幣殿と4棟1組は外玉垣の内側に入った<ref>福山ほか（1975）：228 - 229ページ</ref>。当時は神明造ではなく[[校倉造]]で、千木・鰹木はなかったようである<ref name="ss9">福山ほか（1975）：229ページ</ref>。正宮の敷地内にあったので式年遷宮のたびに移動していたが、中世の末に4棟とも廃絶した<ref name="ss9"/>。
御稲奉下は[[禰宜]]・大内人ら4名が大物忌（おおものいみ）と大物忌父を伴って執行していた<ref name="gs"/>。この時の[[服装]]は、内宮で祭儀を行うのと同じ官給の明衣（きよぎぬ）であり、如何に重要な神社であったかが分かる<ref name="gs"/>。奉下されたイネは大物忌の子らは[[臼]]で撞き、[[竈]]（かまど）で蒸し、炊き上がった[[ご飯]]を[[笥]]（け）に盛って神前に供された<ref name="gs2"/>。御飯は内宮・荒祭宮・[[滝祭神]]に捧げられた<ref name="gs2"/>。
[[天正]]年間（[[1573年]] - [[1592年]]）に4棟のうち、正宮の北西に御稲御倉のみ神明造で復興され、以後式年遷宮ごとに建て替えられた<ref name="ss9"/>。室町時代以降は御稲御倉で[[神嘗祭]]の織御衣（おりのみそ）がここで織られたので、御機殿の異名を持っていた<ref name="ss9"/>。
[[明治]]以降、御稲御倉神が祀られるようになり、[[1889年]]（明治22年）には荒祭宮へ向かう道の途中に鎮座位置が固定された<ref name="ss9"/>。
== 交通 == ; [[宇治橋 (伊勢市)|宇治橋]]前まで * [[東海旅客鉄道|JR]][[参宮線]]・[[近畿日本鉄道|近鉄]][[近鉄山田線|山田線]][[伊勢市駅]]南口（JR側）または近鉄山田線・[[近鉄鳥羽線|鳥羽線]][[宇治山田駅]]より三重交通バスに15分ほど乗車、終点「内宮前」下車<ref name="mkr">三重県観光連盟"[http://www.kankomie.or.jp/spot/detail_2937.html 伊勢神宮（内宮）の観光施設・周辺情報 - 観光三重]"（2013年10月12日閲覧。）</ref>。近鉄鳥羽線[[五十鈴川駅]]からも三重交通バスで内宮前へ行くことが可能である。 * [[伊勢自動車道]][[伊勢西インターチェンジ|伊勢西IC]]より[[三重県道32号伊勢磯部線]]（御木本道路）を南へ約5分または[[伊勢インターチェンジ|伊勢IC]]より[[国道23号]]を南へ約5分、付近に[[駐車場]]あり<ref name="mkr"/>。 ; 宇治橋前から * 宇治橋から内宮（正宮）まで参道を進み、内宮の西側から荒祭宮へ向かう参道を進むと御稲御倉が鎮座する<ref name="ic"/>。付近には外幣殿（げへいでん）もある<ref name="ic"/>。
== 脚注 == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist|2}}
== 参考文献 == * [[伊勢文化舎]] 編『お伊勢さん125社めぐり』別冊『伊勢人』、伊勢文化舎、平成20年12月23日、151p. ISBN 978-4-900759-37-4 * 伊勢文化舎 編『いせびとニュース第12号』残暑号、伊勢文化舎・伊勢市観光協会・おかげ参り推進委員会、平成25年8月17日、8p. * 宇治山田市役所 編『宇治山田市史 上巻』宇治山田市役所、昭和4年1月20日、862p. * [[学研パブリッシング]]『伊勢神宮に行こう』Gakken Mook神社紀行セレクションvol.1、[[薗田稔]]監修、学研マーケティング、2013年7月4日、82p. ISBN 978-4-05-610047-1 * [[櫻井勝之進]]『伊勢神宮』[[学生社]]、昭和45年10月20日重版、251p. * [[福山敏男]]・[[稲垣栄三|稲垣榮三]]・村瀬美樹・胡麻鶴醇之『神宮―第六十回神宮式年遷宮―』[[小学館]]、昭和50年4月20日、246p. * 矢野憲一『伊勢神宮―知られざる杜のうち』角川選書402、[[角川学芸出版]]、平成18年11月10日、270p. ISBN 4-04-703402-9
== 関連項目 == {{Commonscat|Mishinenomikura}} * [[神宮125社の一覧]]
== 外部リンク == * [http://www.isejingu.or.jp/about/naiku/others.html#mishinenomikura 御稲御倉] - 伊勢神宮 * [http://www.jingukaikan.jp/125mairi/m01/0113.html 御稲御倉（みしねのみくら）] - 財団法人伊勢神宮崇敬会 {{神宮125社}} {{神道 横}} {{デフォルトソート:みしねのみくら}} [[Category:神宮125社]] [[Category:日本の倉庫]] [[Category:伊勢市の神社]] [[Category:2013年竣工の日本の建築物]]

revision.comment: Immanuelle moved page [[御稲御倉]] to [[Mishine-no-Mikura]] without leaving a redirect: Bot: merge import
revision.text:
{{神社 |名称= 御稲御倉 |画像= [[ファイル:Mishine no Mikura.jpg|270px]] |所在地 = [[三重県]][[伊勢市]]宇治館町1 内宮宮域内 |緯度度 = 34 | 緯度分 = 27 | 緯度秒 = 18.8 |経度度 = 136 |経度分 = 43 | 経度秒 = 28.7 |地図= Japan Mie |ラベル位置=bottom |祭神= 御稲御倉神 |社格= [[皇大神宮]]所管社 |創建= [[古代]] |本殿= [[唯一神明造]] |別名= |札所等= |例祭= |神事= [[神嘗祭]] }}
'''御稲御倉'''（みしねのみくら、ごとうのみくら<ref name="ss8">福山ほか（1975）：228ページ</ref>）は、[[伊勢神宮]][[皇大神宮]]（内宮）の所管社。'''御稲御倉神'''（みしねのみくらのかみ）とも称する<ref name="ic">伊勢文化舎 編（2008）：114ページ</ref><ref>宇治山田市役所 編（1929）：16ページ</ref><ref name="gs2">櫻井（1970）：156ページ</ref>。内宮の宮域内、[[荒祭宮]]に向かう道の途中に鎮座する<ref name="ic"/>。
小さいながら、内宮と同じ[[唯一神明造]]の[[神社]]である<ref name="ic"/><ref name="gk">学研パブリッシング（2013）：56ページ</ref>。
== 概要 == [[三重県]][[伊勢市]]宇治館町、内宮の宮域、正宮の北西に鎮座する<ref name="gk"/>。内宮の所管社30社のうち第6位である。内宮の正宮で[[祭]]が行われるたびに御稲御倉でも[[祭祀]]が行われる<ref name="ic"/>。御稲御倉の北には外幣殿（げへいでん）がある<ref name="ss8"/>。
神宮神田で収穫された[[イネ]]は抜穂（ぬいぼ）にして、御稲御倉へ納められる<ref name="ic"/>。御倉のイネは内宮の祭祀に合わせて取り出され、[[神饌|大御饌]]として[[アマテラス|天照大神]]に捧げられる<ref name="ic"/><ref name="gk"/>。すなわち内宮[[穀倉]]として利用されている<ref name="gk"/>。古式に則り、[[装束]]をまとった[[神職]]が御倉へ納める<ref name="gs">櫻井（1970）：155ページ</ref>。御倉からイネを取り出すことを御稲奉下（ごとうほうげ<ref name="gs"/>、みしねほうげ<ref name="sy">矢野（2006）：111ページ</ref>）と称し、前日から参籠潔斎（さんろうけっさい）した神職が行う<ref name="gs"/>。奉下されたイネは稲扱（いねこき）、籾摺（もみすり）、七分搗きにした後、忌火屋殿で[[神酒]]・[[餅|御餅]]・御飯（おんいい）に調理され、神前に供される<ref name="sy"/>。
なお、[[神宮式年遷宮]]の祭事の1つで、正殿の床下に柱を立てる「心御柱奉建」（しんのみはしらほうけん）で用いる心御柱も御稲御倉に納められる<ref>{{Cite news|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20130926070226/http://sankei.jp.msn.com/life/news/130925/trd13092522350017-n1.htm|title=内宮で心御柱奉建 伊勢神宮式年遷宮|publisher=[[産経新聞]]|work=MSN産経ニュース|date=2013年9月25日|accessdate=2013年10月13日|archiveurl=https://megalodon.jp/2013-1013-0152-17/sankei.jp.msn.com/life/news/130925/trd13092522350017-n1.htm|archivedate=2013年10月13日}}</ref>。
[[社殿]]は唯一神明造である<ref name="ic"/>。内宮の方角（東）を向いて建っている<ref name="ic"/>。[[高床建物|高床]]の[[切妻]]屋根で、[[千木・鰹木]]を備えるなど、内宮の東西宝殿や外幣殿に酷似した構造である<ref name="ss8"/>。高床であるため、御稲奉下の際には取り外し可能な階段を取り付け、[[権禰宜]]が御鑰（みかぎ）を開錠して御扉を開き、イネを取り出す<ref>矢野（2006）：111 - 112ページ</ref>。[[玉垣]]や[[賽銭]]箱はない。
== 祭神 == [[祭神]]は御稲御倉神（みしねのみくらのかみ）<ref name="ic"/>。御稲御倉の[[守護神]]である<ref name="ic"/><ref name="gk"/>。[[神宮式年遷宮]]に伴って社殿が建て替えられる際には、御稲御倉神は一時的に外幣殿に遷座する<ref>伊勢文化舎 編（2013）：6ページ</ref>。
[[平安時代]]の『大治御形記』には[[保食神]]（うけもちのかみ）とあり、[[鎌倉時代]]の『神名秘書』では[[ウカノミタマ]]とする<ref name="gs P155-156">櫻井（1970）：155 - 156ページ</ref>。
== 歴史 == [[古代]]より存在したと考えられる<ref name="gs"/>。調御倉（つきのみくら）・御塩御倉・鋪設御倉・御稲御倉・御稲御倉の4棟で1組として板垣外の西方に建っていたが、[[平安時代]]中頃より、外幣殿と4棟1組は外玉垣の内側に入った<ref>福山ほか（1975）：228 - 229ページ</ref>。当時は神明造ではなく[[校倉造]]で、千木・鰹木はなかったようである<ref name="ss9">福山ほか（1975）：229ページ</ref>。正宮の敷地内にあったので式年遷宮のたびに移動していたが、中世の末に4棟とも廃絶した<ref name="ss9"/>。
御稲奉下は[[禰宜]]・大内人ら4名が大物忌（おおものいみ）と大物忌父を伴って執行していた<ref name="gs"/>。この時の[[服装]]は、内宮で祭儀を行うのと同じ官給の明衣（きよぎぬ）であり、如何に重要な神社であったかが分かる<ref name="gs"/>。奉下されたイネは大物忌の子らは[[臼]]で撞き、[[竈]]（かまど）で蒸し、炊き上がった[[ご飯]]を[[笥]]（け）に盛って神前に供された<ref name="gs2"/>。御飯は内宮・荒祭宮・[[滝祭神]]に捧げられた<ref name="gs2"/>。
[[天正]]年間（[[1573年]] - [[1592年]]）に4棟のうち、正宮の北西に御稲御倉のみ神明造で復興され、以後式年遷宮ごとに建て替えられた<ref name="ss9"/>。室町時代以降は御稲御倉で[[神嘗祭]]の織御衣（おりのみそ）がここで織られたので、御機殿の異名を持っていた<ref name="ss9"/>。
[[明治]]以降、御稲御倉神が祀られるようになり、[[1889年]]（明治22年）には荒祭宮へ向かう道の途中に鎮座位置が固定された<ref name="ss9"/>。
== 交通 == ; [[宇治橋 (伊勢市)|宇治橋]]前まで * [[東海旅客鉄道|JR]][[参宮線]]・[[近畿日本鉄道|近鉄]][[近鉄山田線|山田線]][[伊勢市駅]]南口（JR側）または近鉄山田線・[[近鉄鳥羽線|鳥羽線]][[宇治山田駅]]より三重交通バスに15分ほど乗車、終点「内宮前」下車<ref name="mkr">三重県観光連盟"[http://www.kankomie.or.jp/spot/detail_2937.html 伊勢神宮（内宮）の観光施設・周辺情報 - 観光三重]"（2013年10月12日閲覧。）</ref>。近鉄鳥羽線[[五十鈴川駅]]からも三重交通バスで内宮前へ行くことが可能である。 * [[伊勢自動車道]][[伊勢西インターチェンジ|伊勢西IC]]より[[三重県道32号伊勢磯部線]]（御木本道路）を南へ約5分または[[伊勢インターチェンジ|伊勢IC]]より[[国道23号]]を南へ約5分、付近に[[駐車場]]あり<ref name="mkr"/>。 ; 宇治橋前から * 宇治橋から内宮（正宮）まで参道を進み、内宮の西側から荒祭宮へ向かう参道を進むと御稲御倉が鎮座する<ref name="ic"/>。付近には外幣殿（げへいでん）もある<ref name="ic"/>。

ISBN 978-4-05-610047-1 * [[櫻井勝之進]]『伊勢神宮』[[学生社]]、昭和45年10月20日重版、251p. * [[福山敏男]]・[[稲垣栄三|稲垣榮三]]・村瀬美樹・胡麻鶴醇之『神宮―第六十回神宮式年遷宮―』[[小学館]]、昭和50年4月20日、246p. * 矢野憲一『伊勢神宮―知られざる杜のうち』角川選書402、[[角川学芸出版]]、平成18年11月10日、270p. ISBN 4-04-703402-9
