{{Shrine |Name = Mishine no Mikura |religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Mishine no Mikura.jpg|270px]] |location = {{ill|Mie Prefecture|ja|三重県}}, {{ill|Ise, Mie|ja|伊勢市}}, Ujikan-cho 1, within the precincts of the Inner Shrine |Latitude Degrees = 34 | Latitude Minutes = 27 | Latitude Seconds = 18.8 |Longitude Degrees = 136 | Longitude Minutes = 43 | Longitude Seconds = 28.7 |Map = Japan Mie |Label Position = bottom |deity = Mishine no Mikura no Kami |type = {{ill|Kōtai Jingū|ja|皇大神宮|1=Kōtai Jingū|2=ja|3=皇大神宮|4=zh|5=皇大神宮}} auxiliary shrine |Established = Ancient times |Main Hall = {{ill|Yuitsu Shimmei-zukuri|ja|唯一神明造}} |Other Names = |Pilgrimage Sites = |festival = |Rituals = {{ill|Kannamesai|ja|神嘗祭}} }}
'''Mishine no Mikura''' (御稲御倉, also read as *Gotō no Mikura*<ref name="ss8">Fukuyama et al. (1975): p. 228</ref>) is an auxiliary shrine under the jurisdiction of {{ill|Kōtai Jingū|ja|皇大神宮|1=Kōtai Jingū|2=ja|3=皇大神宮|4=zh|5=皇大神宮}} (the Inner Shrine) of {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮}}. It is also referred to as '''Mishine no Mikura no Kami''' (御稲御倉神)<ref name="ic">Ise Bunka-sha, ed. (2008): p. 114</ref><ref>Ujiyamada City Office, ed. (1929): p. 16</ref><ref name="gs2">Sakurai (1970): p. 156</ref>. The shrine is located within the precincts of the Inner Shrine, along the path leading to {{ill|Aramatsuri-no-Miya|ja|荒祭宮}}<ref name="ic"/>.
Although small, it features the same {{ill|Yuitsu Shimmei-zukuri|ja|唯一神明造}} architectural style as the Inner Shrine<ref name="ic"/><ref name="gk">Gakken Publishing (2013): p. 56</ref>.
== Overview ==
Located in {{ill|Ujikanda-chō|ja|宇治館町}}, {{ill|Ise City|ja|伊勢市}}, Mie Prefecture, this shrine stands northwest of the main sanctuary (Shōgu) within the precincts of the {{ill|Naikū|ja|内宮}}<ref name="gk"/>. It Ranks 6th among the 30 shrines under the jurisdiction of Naikū. Each time a festival is held at the main sanctuary of Naikū, a ritual is also conducted at the {{ill|Mishine-no-Mikura|ja|御稲御倉}}<ref name="ic"/>. To the north of Mishine-no-Mikura is the {{ill|Geheiden|ja|外幣殿}}<ref name="ss8"/>.
The {{ill|rice|ja|イネ}} harvested from the sacred fields of Ise Jingu is presented as {{ill|nuiho|ja|抜穂}} and stored in Mishine-no-Mikura<ref name="ic"/>. This rice is later retrieved in accordance with Naikū’s rituals and offered as {{ill|Ōmikue|ja|大御饌}} to {{ill|Amaterasu|ja|天照大神|1=Amaterasu|lt=Amaterasu Ōmikami}}<ref name="ic"/><ref name="gk"/>. Essentially, it functions as the granary of Naikū<ref name="gk"/>. Following ancient customs, Shinto priests dressed in traditional garments deliver the rice to the Mikura<ref name="gs">Sakurai (1970): p.155</ref>.
The act of retrieving rice from the Mikura is called either {{ill|Gotō Hōge|ja|御稲奉下}} (Gotō Hōge<ref name="gs"/>, Mishine Hōge<ref name="sy">Yano (2006): p.111</ref>). It is carried out by priests who have undergone purification rites the day before<ref name="gs"/>. After retrieval, the rice undergoes threshing, dehusking, and polishing to about 70%. It is then prepared into {{ill|sacred sake|ja|神酒}}, {{ill|mochi|ja|餅}}, and cooked rice ({{ill|Onii|ja|御飯}}) in the {{ill|Imibiya-den|ja|忌火屋殿}}, and offered before the deities<ref name="sy"/>.
Additionally, during the {{ill|Jingū Shikinen Sengū|ja|神宮式年遷宮}}, one of the key rituals involves the placement of the central sacred pillar ({{ill|Shin-no-Mihashira|ja|心御柱}}), which is temporarily stored in Mishine-no-Mikura<ref>{{Cite news|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20130926070226/http://sankei.jp.msn.com/life/news/130925/trd13092522350017-n1.htm|title=Shin-no-Mihashira Ceremony at Naikū for Jingū Shikinen Sengū|publisher=Sankei Shimbun|work=MSN Sankei News|date=September 25, 2013|accessdate=October 13, 2013}}</ref>.
The shrine building is constructed in the {{ill|Yuitsu-shinmei-zukuri|ja|唯一神明造}} style<ref name="ic"/>. It faces east toward Naikū<ref name="ic"/>. The elevated structure with a {{ill|kirizuma-style|ja|切妻}} roof and decorative {{ill|chigi and katsuogi|ja|千木・鰹木}} resembles the East and West Treasuries of Naikū and the Geheiden<ref name="ss8"/>. Due to its elevated design, a removable staircase is attached during rice retrieval. The {{ill|Gon-negi|ja|権禰宜}} unlocks the door with a sacred key ({{ill|Mikagi|ja|御鑰}}) to access the rice<ref>Yano (2006): pp.111–112</ref>. There are no surrounding {{ill|tamagaki|ja|玉垣}} fences or offering boxes.
== Enshrined deity ==
The enshrined deity is Mishine-no-Mikura-no-Kami (御稲御倉神)<ref name="ic"/>. This deity serves as the {{ill|guardian deity|ja|守護神}} of Mishine-no-Mikura<ref name="ic"/><ref name="gk"/>. During the {{ill|Jingū Shikinen Sengū|ja|神宮式年遷宮}}, when the shrine is rebuilt, Mishine-no-Mikura-no-Kami is temporarily transferred to the Geheiden<ref>Ise Bunka-sha (2013): p.6</ref>.
In the Heian-period text "Daiji Mikagami," the deity is referred to as {{ill|Ukemochi-no-Kami|ja|保食神|1=Ukemochi}}, while in the Kamakura-period "Jinmyō Hisho," it is identified as {{ill|Ukanomitama|ja|ウカノミタマ}}<ref name="gs P155-156">Sakurai (1970): pp.155–156</ref>.
== History ==
It is believed to have existed since ancient times<ref name="gs"/>. Originally, it was part of a complex of four granaries—Tsuki-no-Mikura, Mishio-no-Mikura, Fushiki-no-Mikura, and Mishine-no-Mikura—located west of the outer fence. By the mid-Heian period, these granaries, along with the Geheiden, were moved inside the outer sacred fence<ref>Fukuyama et al. (1975): pp.228–229</ref>. At the time, the structure was built in the {{ill|azekura-zukuri|ja|校倉造}} style without chigi and katsuogi<ref name="ss9">Fukuyama et al. (1975): p.229</ref>. As it was within the grounds of the main sanctuary, it was relocated with each Shikinen Sengū, but all four granaries were abolished by the end of the medieval period<ref name="ss9"/>.
The rice retrieval ceremony was conducted by four officials, including {{ill|negi|ja|禰宜}} and Ōuchi-hito, accompanied by Ōmonoimi (great purification officials)<ref name="gs"/>. Their attire consisted of the same sacred garments used during Naikū rituals, indicating the shrine's significance<ref name="gs"/>. The retrieved rice was milled, steamed, and served in sacred vessels ({{ill|ke|ja|笥}}) before the deities<ref name="gs2"/>. The offerings were dedicated to Naikū, {{ill|Aramatsuri-no-Miya|ja|荒祭宮}}, and {{ill|Takimatsuri-no-Kami|ja|滝deity}}<ref name="gs2"/>.
During the {{ill|Tenshō era|ja|天正|1=Tenshō (Momoyama period)|lt=Tenshō Era}} (1573–1592), Mishine-no-Mikura was restored in the Yuitsu-shinmei-zukuri style northwest of the main sanctuary and has since been rebuilt with each Shikinen Sengū<ref name="ss9"/>. After the Muromachi period, it was also known as the "Sacred Weaving Hall" (御機殿) due to its association with sacred weaving during the {{ill|Kannamesai|ja|神嘗祭}}<ref name="ss9"/>.
Since the Meiji period, Mishine-no-Mikura-no-Kami has been formally enshrined, and in 1889, the shrine’s location was fixed along the path leading to Aramatsuri-no-Miya<ref name="ss9"/>.
== Access ==
; To Ujibashi Bridge * About 15 minutes by {{ill|Mie Kotsu|ja|三重交通}} bus from the south exit (JR side) of {{ill|Ise Station|ja|伊勢市駅}} on the {{ill|JR Sangū Line|ja|参宮線}} or from {{ill|Ujiyamada Station|ja|Ujiyamada Station}} on the Kintetsu Yamada and Toba Lines, alighting at the final stop "Naikū-mae"<ref name="mkr">Mie Prefecture Tourism Federation, "[http://www.kankomie.or.jp/spot/detail_2937.html Ise Jingu (Naikū) Tourist Info - Visit Mie]" (Accessed October 12, 2013)</ref>. Also accessible via bus from {{ill|Isuzugawa Station|ja|五十鈴川駅}} on the Kintetsu Toba Line. * About 5 minutes south via {{ill|Mie Prefectural Route 32|ja|三重県道32号伊勢磯部線}} (Mikimoto Road) from {{ill|Ise-nishi Interchange|ja|伊勢西インターチェンジ}} on the {{ill|Ise Expressway|ja|伊勢自動車道}}, or from {{ill|Ise Interchange|ja|伊勢インターチェンジ}} via {{ill|National Route 23 (Japan)|ja|国道23号}}. Parking available nearby<ref name="mkr"/>.
; From Ujibashi Bridge * Proceed along the path from Ujibashi toward the main sanctuary of Naikū, then take the path leading west toward Aramatsuri-no-Miya, where Mishine-no-Mikura is enshrined<ref name="ic"/>. The nearby Geheiden can also be found here<ref name="ic"/>.

{{Shrine |Name = Mishine no Mikura |religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Mishine no Mikura.jpg|270px]] |location = {{ill|Mie Prefecture|ja|三重県|12=draft}}, {{ill|Ise, Mie|ja|伊勢市|12=draft}}, Ujikan-cho 1, within the precincts of the Inner Shrine |Latitude Degrees = 34 | Latitude Minutes = 27 | Latitude Seconds = 18.8 |Longitude Degrees = 136 | Longitude Minutes = 43 | Longitude Seconds = 28.7 |Map = Japan Mie |Label Position = bottom |deity = Mishine no Mikura no Kami |type = {{ill|Kōtai Jingū|ja|皇大神宮|1=Kōtai Jingū|2=ja|3=皇大神宮|4=zh|5=皇大神宮|12=draft}} auxiliary shrine |Established = Ancient times |Main Hall = {{ill|Yuitsu Shimmei-zukuri|ja|唯一神明造|12=draft}} |Other Names = |Pilgrimage Sites = |festival = |Rituals = {{ill|Kannamesai|ja|神嘗祭|12=draft}} }}
'''Mishine no Mikura''' (御稲御倉, also read as *Gotō no Mikura*<ref name="ss8">Fukuyama et al. (1975): p. 228</ref>) is an auxiliary shrine under the jurisdiction of {{ill|Kōtai Jingū|ja|皇大神宮|1=Kōtai Jingū|2=ja|3=皇大神宮|4=zh|5=皇大神宮|12=draft}} (the Inner Shrine) of {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮|12=draft}}. It is also referred to as '''Mishine no Mikura no Kami''' (御稲御倉神)<ref name="ic">Ise Bunka-sha, ed. (2008): p. 114</ref><ref>Ujiyamada City Office, ed. (1929): p. 16</ref><ref name="gs2">Sakurai (1970): p. 156</ref>. The shrine is located within the precincts of the Inner Shrine, along the path leading to {{ill|Aramatsuri-no-Miya|ja|荒祭宮|12=draft}}<ref name="ic"/>.
Although small, it features the same {{ill|Yuitsu Shimmei-zukuri|ja|唯一神明造|12=draft}} architectural style as the Inner Shrine<ref name="ic"/><ref name="gk">Gakken Publishing (2013): p. 56</ref>.
== Overview ==
Located in {{ill|Ujikanda-chō|ja|宇治館町|12=draft}}, {{ill|Ise City|ja|伊勢市|12=draft}}, Mie Prefecture, this shrine stands northwest of the main sanctuary (Shōgu) within the precincts of the {{ill|Naikū|ja|内宮|12=draft}}<ref name="gk"/>. It Ranks 6th among the 30 shrines under the jurisdiction of Naikū. Each time a festival is held at the main sanctuary of Naikū, a ritual is also conducted at the {{ill|Mishine-no-Mikura|ja|御稲御倉|12=draft}}<ref name="ic"/>. To the north of Mishine-no-Mikura is the {{ill|Geheiden|ja|外幣殿|12=draft}}<ref name="ss8"/>.
The {{ill|rice|ja|イネ|12=draft}} harvested from the sacred fields of Ise Jingu is presented as {{ill|nuiho|ja|抜穂|12=draft}} and stored in Mishine-no-Mikura<ref name="ic"/>. This rice is later retrieved in accordance with Naikū’s rituals and offered as {{ill|Ōmikue|ja|大御饌|12=draft}} to {{ill|Amaterasu|ja|天照大神|1=Amaterasu|lt=Amaterasu Ōmikami|12=draft}}<ref name="ic"/><ref name="gk"/>. Essentially, it functions as the granary of Naikū<ref name="gk"/>. Following ancient customs, Shinto priests dressed in traditional garments deliver the rice to the Mikura<ref name="gs">Sakurai (1970): p.155</ref>.
The act of retrieving rice from the Mikura is called either {{ill|Gotō Hōge|ja|御稲奉下|12=draft}} (Gotō Hōge<ref name="gs"/>, Mishine Hōge<ref name="sy">Yano (2006): p.111</ref>). It is carried out by priests who have undergone purification rites the day before<ref name="gs"/>. After retrieval, the rice undergoes threshing, dehusking, and polishing to about 70%. It is then prepared into {{ill|sacred sake|ja|神酒|12=draft}}, {{ill|mochi|ja|餅|12=draft}}, and cooked rice ({{ill|Onii|ja|御飯|12=draft}}) in the {{ill|Imibiya-den|ja|忌火屋殿|12=draft}}, and offered before the deities<ref name="sy"/>.
Additionally, during the {{ill|Jingū Shikinen Sengū|ja|神宮式年遷宮|12=draft}}, one of the key rituals involves the placement of the central sacred pillar ({{ill|Shin-no-Mihashira|ja|心御柱|12=draft}}), which is temporarily stored in Mishine-no-Mikura<ref>{{Cite news|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20130926070226/http://sankei.jp.msn.com/life/news/130925/trd13092522350017-n1.htm|title=Shin-no-Mihashira Ceremony at Naikū for Jingū Shikinen Sengū|publisher=Sankei Shimbun|work=MSN Sankei News|date=September 25, 2013|accessdate=October 13, 2013}}</ref>.
The shrine building is constructed in the {{ill|Yuitsu-shinmei-zukuri|ja|唯一神明造|12=draft}} style<ref name="ic"/>. It faces east toward Naikū<ref name="ic"/>. The elevated structure with a {{ill|kirizuma-style|ja|切妻|12=draft}} roof and decorative {{ill|chigi and katsuogi|ja|千木・鰹木|12=draft}} resembles the East and West Treasuries of Naikū and the Geheiden<ref name="ss8"/>. Due to its elevated design, a removable staircase is attached during rice retrieval. The {{ill|Gon-negi|ja|権禰宜|12=draft}} unlocks the door with a sacred key ({{ill|Mikagi|ja|御鑰|12=draft}}) to access the rice<ref>Yano (2006): pp.111–112</ref>. There are no surrounding {{ill|tamagaki|ja|玉垣|12=draft}} fences or offering boxes.
== Enshrined deity ==
The enshrined deity is Mishine-no-Mikura-no-Kami (御稲御倉神)<ref name="ic"/>. This deity serves as the {{ill|guardian deity|ja|守護神|12=draft}} of Mishine-no-Mikura<ref name="ic"/><ref name="gk"/>. During the {{ill|Jingū Shikinen Sengū|ja|神宮式年遷宮|12=draft}}, when the shrine is rebuilt, Mishine-no-Mikura-no-Kami is temporarily transferred to the Geheiden<ref>Ise Bunka-sha (2013): p.6</ref>.
In the Heian-period text "Daiji Mikagami," the deity is referred to as {{ill|Ukemochi-no-Kami|ja|保食神|1=Ukemochi|12=draft}}, while in the Kamakura-period "Jinmyō Hisho," it is identified as {{ill|Ukanomitama|ja|ウカノミタマ|12=draft}}<ref name="gs P155-156">Sakurai (1970): pp.155–156</ref>.
== History ==
It is believed to have existed since ancient times<ref name="gs"/>. Originally, it was part of a complex of four granaries—Tsuki-no-Mikura, Mishio-no-Mikura, Fushiki-no-Mikura, and Mishine-no-Mikura—located west of the outer fence. By the mid-Heian period, these granaries, along with the Geheiden, were moved inside the outer sacred fence<ref>Fukuyama et al. (1975): pp.228–229</ref>. At the time, the structure was built in the {{ill|azekura-zukuri|ja|校倉造|12=draft}} style without chigi and katsuogi<ref name="ss9">Fukuyama et al. (1975): p.229</ref>. As it was within the grounds of the main sanctuary, it was relocated with each Shikinen Sengū, but all four granaries were abolished by the end of the medieval period<ref name="ss9"/>.
The rice retrieval ceremony was conducted by four officials, including {{ill|negi|ja|禰宜|12=draft}} and Ōuchi-hito, accompanied by Ōmonoimi (great purification officials)<ref name="gs"/>. Their attire consisted of the same sacred garments used during Naikū rituals, indicating the shrine's significance<ref name="gs"/>. The retrieved rice was milled, steamed, and served in sacred vessels ({{ill|ke|ja|笥|12=draft}}) before the deities<ref name="gs2"/>. The offerings were dedicated to Naikū, {{ill|Aramatsuri-no-Miya|ja|荒祭宮|12=draft}}, and {{ill|Takimatsuri-no-Kami|ja|滝deity|12=draft}}<ref name="gs2"/>.
During the {{ill|Tenshō era|ja|天正|1=Tenshō (Momoyama period)|lt=Tenshō Era|12=draft}} (1573–1592), Mishine-no-Mikura was restored in the Yuitsu-shinmei-zukuri style northwest of the main sanctuary and has since been rebuilt with each Shikinen Sengū<ref name="ss9"/>. After the Muromachi period, it was also known as the "Sacred Weaving Hall" (御機殿) due to its association with sacred weaving during the {{ill|Kannamesai|ja|神嘗祭|12=draft}}<ref name="ss9"/>.
Since the Meiji period, Mishine-no-Mikura-no-Kami has been formally enshrined, and in 1889, the shrine’s location was fixed along the path leading to Aramatsuri-no-Miya<ref name="ss9"/>.

; To Ujibashi Bridge * About 15 minutes by {{ill|Mie Kotsu|ja|三重交通|12=draft}} bus from the south exit (JR side) of {{ill|Ise Station|ja|伊勢市駅|12=draft}} on the {{ill|JR Sangū Line|ja|参宮線|12=draft}} or from {{ill|Ujiyamada Station|ja|Ujiyamada Station|12=draft}} on the Kintetsu Yamada and Toba Lines, alighting at the final stop "Naikū-mae"<ref name="mkr">Mie Prefecture Tourism Federation, "[http://www.kankomie.or.jp/spot/detail_2937.html Ise Jingu (Naikū) Tourist Info - Visit Mie]" (Accessed October 12, 2013)</ref>. Also accessible via bus from {{ill|Isuzugawa Station|ja|五十鈴川駅|12=draft}} on the Kintetsu Toba Line. * About 5 minutes south via {{ill|Mie Prefectural Route 32|ja|三重県道32号伊勢磯部線|12=draft}} (Mikimoto Road) from {{ill|Ise-nishi Interchange|ja|伊勢西インターチェンジ|12=draft}} on the {{ill|Ise Expressway|ja|伊勢自動車道|12=draft}}, or from {{ill|Ise Interchange|ja|伊勢インターチェンジ|12=draft}} via {{ill|National Route 23 (Japan)|ja|国道23号|12=draft}}. Parking available nearby<ref name="mkr"/>.
; From Ujibashi Bridge * Proceed along the path from Ujibashi toward the main sanctuary of Naikū, then take the path leading west toward Aramatsuri-no-Miya, where Mishine-no-Mikura is enshrined<ref name="ic"/>. The nearby Geheiden can also be found here<ref name="ic"/>.

It is carried out by priests who have undergone purification rites the day before<ref name="gs"/>. After retrieval, the rice undergoes threshing, dehusking, and polishing to about 70%. It is then prepared into {{ill|sacred sake|ja|神酒|qq=draft}}, {{ill|mochi|ja|餅|qq=draft}}, and cooked rice ({{ill|Onii|ja|御飯|qq=draft}}) in the {{ill|Imibiya-den|ja|忌火屋殿|qq=draft}}, and offered before the deities<ref name="sy"/>.
Additionally, during the {{ill|Jingū Shikinen Sengū|ja|神宮式年遷宮|qq=draft}}, one of the key rituals involves the placement of the central sacred pillar ({{ill|Shin-no-Mihashira|ja|心御柱|qq=draft}}), which is temporarily stored in Mishine-no-Mikura<ref>{{Cite news|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20130926070226/http://sankei.jp.msn.com/life/news/130925/trd13092522350017-n1.htm|title=Shin-no-Mihashira Ceremony at Naikū for Jingū Shikinen Sengū|publisher=Sankei Shimbun|work=MSN Sankei News|date=September 25, 2013|accessdate=October 13, 2013}}</ref>.
The shrine building is constructed in the {{ill|Yuitsu-shinmei-zukuri|ja|唯一神明造|qq=draft}} style<ref name="ic"/>. It faces east toward Naikū<ref name="ic"/>. The elevated structure with a {{ill|kirizuma-style|ja|切妻|qq=draft}} roof and decorative {{ill|chigi and katsuogi|ja|千木・鰹木|qq=draft}} resembles the East and West Treasuries of Naikū and the Geheiden<ref name="ss8"/>. Due to its elevated design, a removable staircase is attached during rice retrieval. The {{ill|Gon-negi|ja|権禰宜|qq=draft}} unlocks the door with a sacred key ({{ill|Mikagi|ja|御鑰|qq=draft}}) to access the rice<ref>Yano (2006): pp.111–112</ref>. There are no surrounding {{ill|tamagaki|ja|玉垣|qq=draft}} fences or offering boxes.
== Enshrined deity ==
The enshrined deity is Mishine-no-Mikura-no-Kami (御稲御倉神)<ref name="ic"/>. This deity serves as the {{ill|guardian deity|ja|守護神|qq=draft}} of Mishine-no-Mikura<ref name="ic"/><ref name="gk"/>. During the {{ill|Jingū Shikinen Sengū|ja|神宮式年遷宮|qq=draft}}, when the shrine is rebuilt, Mishine-no-Mikura-no-Kami is temporarily transferred to the Geheiden<ref>Ise Bunka-sha (2013): p.6</ref>.
In the Heian-period text "Daiji Mikagami," the deity is referred to as {{ill|Ukemochi-no-Kami|ja|保食神|1=Ukemochi|qq=draft}}, while in the Kamakura-period "Jinmyō Hisho," it is identified as {{ill|Ukanomitama|ja|ウカノミタマ|qq=draft}}<ref name="gs P155-156">Sakurai (1970): pp.155–156</ref>.
== History ==
It is believed to have existed since ancient times<ref name="gs"/>. Originally, it was part of a complex of four granaries—Tsuki-no-Mikura, Mishio-no-Mikura, Fushiki-no-Mikura, and Mishine-no-Mikura—located west of the outer fence. By the mid-Heian period, these granaries, along with the Geheiden, were moved inside the outer sacred fence<ref>Fukuyama et al. (1975): pp.228–229</ref>. At the time, the structure was built in the {{ill|azekura-zukuri|ja|校倉造|qq=draft}} style without chigi and katsuogi<ref name="ss9">Fukuyama et al. (1975): p.229</ref>. As it was within the grounds of the main sanctuary, it was relocated with each Shikinen Sengū, but all four granaries were abolished by the end of the medieval period<ref name="ss9"/>.
The rice retrieval ceremony was conducted by four officials, including {{ill|negi|ja|禰宜|qq=draft}} and Ōuchi-hito, accompanied by Ōmonoimi (great purification officials)<ref name="gs"/>. Their attire consisted of the same sacred garments used during Naikū rituals, indicating the shrine's significance<ref name="gs"/>. The retrieved rice was milled, steamed, and served in sacred vessels ({{ill|ke|ja|笥|qq=draft}}) before the deities<ref name="gs2"/>. The offerings were dedicated to Naikū, {{ill|Aramatsuri-no-Miya|ja|荒祭宮|qq=draft}}, and {{ill|Takimatsuri-no-Kami|ja|滝deity|qq=draft}}<ref name="gs2"/>.
During the {{ill|Tenshō era|ja|天正|1=Tenshō (Momoyama period)|lt=Tenshō Era|qq=draft}} (1573–1592), Mishine-no-Mikura was restored in the Yuitsu-shinmei-zukuri style northwest of the main sanctuary and has since been rebuilt with each Shikinen Sengū<ref name="ss9"/>. After the Muromachi period, it was also known as the "Sacred Weaving Hall" (御機殿) due to its association with sacred weaving during the {{ill|Kannamesai|ja|神嘗祭|qq=draft}}<ref name="ss9"/>.
Since the Meiji period, Mishine-no-Mikura-no-Kami has been formally enshrined, and in 1889, the shrine’s location was fixed along the path leading to Aramatsuri-no-Miya<ref name="ss9"/>.
== Access ==
; To Ujibashi Bridge * About 15 minutes by {{ill|Mie Kotsu|ja|三重交通|qq=draft}} bus from the south exit (JR side) of {{ill|Ise Station|ja|伊勢市駅|qq=draft}} on the {{ill|JR Sangū Line|ja|参宮線|qq=draft}} or from {{ill|Ujiyamada Station|ja|Ujiyamada Station|qq=draft}} on the Kintetsu Yamada and Toba Lines, alighting at the final stop "Naikū-mae"<ref name="mkr">Mie Prefecture Tourism Federation, "[http://www.kankomie.or.jp/spot/detail_2937.html Ise Jingu (Naikū) Tourist Info - Visit Mie]" (Accessed October 12, 2013)</ref>. Also accessible via bus from {{ill|Isuzugawa Station|ja|五十鈴川駅|qq=draft}} on the Kintetsu Toba Line. * About 5 minutes south via {{ill|Mie Prefectural Route 32|ja|三重県道32号伊勢磯部線|qq=draft}} (Mikimoto Road) from {{ill|Ise-nishi Interchange|ja|伊勢西インターチェンジ|qq=draft}} on the {{ill|Ise Expressway|ja|伊勢自動車道|qq=draft}}, or from {{ill|Ise Interchange|ja|伊勢インターチェンジ|qq=draft}} via {{ill|National Route 23 (Japan)|ja|国道23号|qq=draft}}. Parking available nearby<ref name="mkr"/>.
; From Ujibashi Bridge * Proceed along the path from Ujibashi toward the main sanctuary of Naikū, then take the path leading west toward Aramatsuri-no-Miya, where Mishine-no-Mikura is enshrined<ref name="ic"/>. The nearby Geheiden can also be found here<ref name="ic"/>.

{{Shrine |Name = Mishine no Mikura |religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Mishine no Mikura.jpg|270px]] |location = {{ill|Mie Prefecture|ja|三重県|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Ise, Mie|ja|伊勢市|qq=draft}}, Ujikan-cho 1, within the precincts of the Inner Shrine |Latitude Degrees = 34 | Latitude Minutes = 27 | Latitude Seconds = 18.8 |Longitude Degrees = 136 | Longitude Minutes = 43 | Longitude Seconds = 28.7 |Map = Japan Mie |Label Position = bottom |deity = Mishine no Mikura no Kami |type = {{ill|Kōtai Jingū|ja|皇大神宮|1=Kōtai Jingū|2=ja|3=皇大神宮|4=zh|5=皇大神宮|qq=draft}} auxiliary shrine |Established = Ancient times |Main Hall = {{ill|Yuitsu Shimmei-zukuri|ja|唯一神明造|qq=draft}} |Other Names = |Pilgrimage Sites = |festival = |Rituals = {{ill|Kannamesai|ja|神嘗祭|qq=draft}} }}
'''Mishine no Mikura''' (御稲御倉, also read as *Gotō no Mikura*<ref name="ss8">Fukuyama et al. (1975): p. 228</ref>) is an auxiliary shrine under the jurisdiction of {{ill|Kōtai Jingū|ja|皇大神宮|1=Kōtai Jingū|2=ja|3=皇大神宮|4=zh|5=皇大神宮|qq=draft}} (the Inner Shrine) of {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮|qq=draft}}. It is also referred to as '''Mishine no Mikura no Kami''' (御稲御倉神)<ref name="ic">Ise Bunka-sha, ed. (2008): p. 114</ref><ref>Ujiyamada City Office, ed. (1929): p. 16</ref><ref name="gs2">Sakurai (1970): p. 156</ref>. The shrine is located within the precincts of the Inner Shrine, along the path leading to {{ill|Aramatsuri-no-Miya|ja|荒祭宮|qq=draft}}<ref name="ic"/>.
Although small, it features the same {{ill|Yuitsu Shimmei-zukuri|ja|唯一神明造|qq=draft}} architectural style as the Inner Shrine<ref name="ic"/><ref name="gk">Gakken Publishing (2013): p. 56</ref>.
== Overview ==
Located in {{ill|Ujikanda-chō|ja|宇治館町|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Ise City|ja|伊勢市|qq=draft}}, Mie Prefecture, this shrine stands northwest of the main sanctuary (Shōgu) within the precincts of the {{ill|Naikū|ja|内宮|qq=draft}}<ref name="gk"/>. It Ranks 6th among the 30 shrines under the jurisdiction of Naikū. Each time a festival is held at the main sanctuary of Naikū, a ritual is also conducted at the {{ill|Mishine-no-Mikura|ja|御稲御倉|qq=draft}}<ref name="ic"/>. To the north of Mishine-no-Mikura is the {{ill|Geheiden|ja|外幣殿|qq=draft}}<ref name="ss8"/>.
The {{ill|rice|ja|イネ|qq=draft}} harvested from the sacred fields of Ise Jingu is presented as {{ill|nuiho|ja|抜穂|qq=draft}} and stored in Mishine-no-Mikura<ref name="ic"/>. This rice is later retrieved in accordance with Naikū’s rituals and offered as {{ill|Ōmikue|ja|大御饌|qq=draft}} to {{ill|Amaterasu|ja|天照大神|1=Amaterasu|lt=Amaterasu Ōmikami|qq=draft}}<ref name="ic"/><ref name="gk"/>. Essentially, it functions as the granary of Naikū<ref name="gk"/>. Following ancient customs, Shinto priests dressed in traditional garments deliver the rice to the Mikura<ref name="gs">Sakurai (1970): p.155</ref>.
The act of retrieving rice from the Mikura is called either {{ill|Gotō Hōge|ja|御稲奉下|qq=draft}} (Gotō Hōge<ref name="gs"/>, Mishine Hōge<ref name="sy">Yano (2006): p.111</ref>). It is carried out by priests who have undergone purification rites the day before<ref name="gs"/>. After retrieval, the rice undergoes threshing, dehusking, and polishing to about 70%. It is then prepared into {{ill|sacred sake|ja|神酒|qq=draft}}, {{ill|mochi|ja|餅|qq=draft}}, and cooked rice ({{ill|Onii|ja|御飯|qq=draft}}) in the {{ill|Imibiya-den|ja|忌火屋殿|qq=draft}}, and offered before the deities<ref name="sy"/>.
Additionally, during the {{ill|Jingū Shikinen Sengū|ja|神宮式年遷宮|qq=draft}}, one of the key rituals involves the placement of the central sacred pillar ({{ill|Shin-no-Mihashira|ja|心御柱|qq=draft}}), which is temporarily stored in Mishine-no-Mikura<ref>{{Cite news|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20130926070226/http://sankei.jp.msn.com/life/news/130925/trd13092522350017-n1.htm|title=Shin-no-Mihashira Ceremony at Naikū for Jingū Shikinen Sengū|publisher=Sankei Shimbun|work=MSN Sankei News|date=September 25, 2013|accessdate=October 13, 2013}}</ref>.
The shrine building is constructed in the {{ill|Yuitsu-shinmei-zukuri|ja|唯一神明造|qq=draft}} style<ref name="ic"/>. It faces east toward Naikū<ref name="ic"/>. The elevated structure with a {{ill|kirizuma-style|ja|切妻|qq=draft}} roof and decorative {{ill|chigi and katsuogi|ja|千木・鰹木|qq=draft}} resembles the East and West Treasuries of Naikū and the Geheiden<ref name="ss8"/>. Due to its elevated design, a removable staircase is attached during rice retrieval. The {{ill|Gon-negi|ja|権禰宜|qq=draft}} unlocks the door with a sacred key ({{ill|Mikagi|ja|御鑰|qq=draft}}) to access the rice<ref>Yano (2006): pp.111–112</ref>. There are no surrounding {{ill|tamagaki|ja|玉垣|qq=draft}} fences or offering boxes.
== Enshrined deity ==
The enshrined deity is Mishine-no-Mikura-no-Kami (御稲御倉神)<ref name="ic"/>. This deity serves as the {{ill|guardian deity|ja|守護神|qq=draft}} of Mishine-no-Mikura<ref name="ic"/><ref name="gk"/>. During the {{ill|Jingū Shikinen Sengū|ja|神宮式年遷宮|qq=draft}}, when the shrine is rebuilt, Mishine-no-Mikura-no-Kami is temporarily transferred to the Geheiden<ref>Ise Bunka-sha (2013): p.6</ref>.
In the Heian-period text "Daiji Mikagami," the deity is referred to as {{ill|Ukemochi-no-Kami|ja|保食神|1=Ukemochi|qq=draft}}, while in the Kamakura-period "Jinmyō Hisho," it is identified as {{ill|Ukanomitama|ja|ウカノミタマ|qq=draft}}<ref name="gs P155-156">Sakurai (1970): pp.155–156</ref>.
== History ==
It is believed to have existed since ancient times<ref name="gs"/>. Originally, it was part of a complex of four granaries—Tsuki-no-Mikura, Mishio-no-Mikura, Fushiki-no-Mikura, and Mishine-no-Mikura—located west of the outer fence. By the mid-Heian period, these granaries, along with the Geheiden, were moved inside the outer sacred fence<ref>Fukuyama et al. (1975): pp.228–229</ref>. At the time, the structure was built in the {{ill|azekura-zukuri|ja|校倉造|qq=draft}} style without chigi and katsuogi<ref name="ss9">Fukuyama et al. (1975): p.229</ref>. As it was within the grounds of the main sanctuary, it was relocated with each Shikinen Sengū, but all four granaries were abolished by the end of the medieval period<ref name="ss9"/>.
The rice retrieval ceremony was conducted by four officials, including {{ill|negi|ja|禰宜|qq=draft}} and Ōuchi-hito, accompanied by Ōmonoimi (great purification officials)<ref name="gs"/>. Their attire consisted of the same sacred garments used during Naikū rituals, indicating the shrine's significance<ref name="gs"/>. The retrieved rice was milled, steamed, and served in sacred vessels ({{ill|ke|ja|笥|qq=draft}}) before the deities<ref name="gs2"/>. The offerings were dedicated to Naikū, {{ill|Aramatsuri-no-Miya|ja|荒祭宮|qq=draft}}, and {{ill|Takimatsuri-no-Kami|ja|滝deity|qq=draft}}<ref name="gs2"/>.
During the {{ill|Tenshō era|ja|天正|1=Tenshō (Momoyama period)|lt=Tenshō Era|qq=draft}} (1573–1592), Mishine-no-Mikura was restored in the Yuitsu-shinmei-zukuri style northwest of the main sanctuary and has since been rebuilt with each Shikinen Sengū<ref name="ss9"/>. After the Muromachi period, it was also known as the "Sacred Weaving Hall" (御機殿) due to its association with sacred weaving during the {{ill|Kannamesai|ja|神嘗祭|qq=draft}}<ref name="ss9"/>.
Since the Meiji period, Mishine-no-Mikura-no-Kami has been formally enshrined, and in 1889, the shrine’s location was fixed along the path leading to Aramatsuri-no-Miya<ref name="ss9"/>.
== Access ==
; To Ujibashi Bridge * About 15 minutes by {{ill|Mie Kotsu|ja|三重交通|qq=draft}} bus from the south exit (JR side) of {{ill|Ise Station|ja|伊勢市駅|qq=draft}} on the {{ill|JR Sangū Line|ja|参宮線|qq=draft}} or from {{ill|Ujiyamada Station|ja|Ujiyamada Station|qq=draft}} on the Kintetsu Yamada and Toba Lines, alighting at the final stop "Naikū-mae"<ref name="mkr">Mie Prefecture Tourism Federation, "[http://www.kankomie.or.jp/spot/detail_2937.html Ise Jingu (Naikū) Tourist Info - Visit Mie]" (Accessed October 12, 2013)</ref>. Also accessible via bus from {{ill|Isuzugawa Station|ja|五十鈴川駅|qq=draft}} on the Kintetsu Toba Line. * About 5 minutes south via {{ill|Mie Prefectural Route 32|ja|三重県道32号伊勢磯部線|qq=draft}} (Mikimoto Road) from {{ill|Ise-nishi Interchange|ja|伊勢西インターチェンジ|qq=draft}} on the {{ill|Ise Expressway|ja|伊勢自動車道|qq=draft}}, or from {{ill|Ise Interchange|ja|伊勢インターチェンジ|qq=draft}} via {{ill|National Route 23 (Japan)|ja|国道23号|qq=draft}}. Parking available nearby<ref name="mkr"/>.
; From Ujibashi Bridge * Proceed along the path from Ujibashi toward the main sanctuary of Naikū, then take the path leading west toward Aramatsuri-no-Miya, where Mishine-no-Mikura is enshrined<ref name="ic"/>. The nearby Geheiden can also be found here<ref name="ic"/>.

(1975): p. 228</ref>) is an auxiliary shrine under the jurisdiction of {{ill|Kōtai Jingū|ja|皇大神宮|1=Kōtai Jingū|2=ja|3=皇大神宮|4=zh|5=皇大神宮|qq=draft}} (the Inner Shrine) of {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮|qq=draft}}. (1929): p. 16</ref><ref name="gs2">Sakurai (1970): p. 156</ref>. The shrine is located within the precincts of the Inner Shrine, along the path leading to {{ill|Aramatsuri-no-Miya|ja|荒祭宮|qq=draft}}<ref name="ic"/>.
Although small, it features the same {{ill|Yuitsu Shimmei-zukuri|ja|唯一神明造|qq=draft}} architectural style as the Inner Shrine<ref name="ic"/><ref name="gk">Gakken Publishing (2013): p. 56</ref>.
== Overview ==
Located in {{ill|Ujikanda-chō|ja|宇治館町|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Ise City|ja|伊勢市|qq=draft}}, Mie Prefecture, this shrine stands northwest of the main sanctuary (Shōgu) within the precincts of the {{ill|Naikū|ja|内宮|qq=draft}}<ref name="gk"/>. It Ranks 6th among the 30 shrines under the jurisdiction of Naikū. Each time a festival is held at the main sanctuary of Naikū, a ritual is also conducted at the {{ill|Mishine-no-Mikura|ja|御稲御倉|qq=draft}}<ref name="ic"/>. To the north of Mishine-no-Mikura is the {{ill|Geheiden|ja|外幣殿|qq=draft}}<ref name="ss8"/>.
The {{ill|rice|ja|イネ|qq=draft}} harvested from the sacred fields of Ise Jingu is presented as {{ill|nuiho|ja|抜穂|qq=draft}} and stored in Mishine-no-Mikura<ref name="ic"/>. This rice is later retrieved in accordance with Naikū’s rituals and offered as {{ill|Ōmikue|ja|大御饌|qq=draft}} to {{ill|Amaterasu|ja|天照大神|1=Amaterasu|lt=Amaterasu Ōmikami|qq=draft}}<ref name="ic"/><ref name="gk"/>. Essentially, it functions as the granary of Naikū<ref name="gk"/>. Following ancient customs, Shinto priests dressed in traditional garments deliver the rice to the Mikura<ref name="gs">Sakurai (1970): p.155</ref>.
The act of retrieving rice from the Mikura is called either {{ill|Gotō Hōge|ja|御稲奉下|qq=draft}} (Gotō Hōge<ref name="gs"/>, Mishine Hōge<ref name="sy">Yano (2006): p.111</ref>). It is carried out by priests who have undergone purification rites the day before<ref name="gs"/>. After retrieval, the rice undergoes threshing, dehusking, and polishing to about 70%. It is then prepared into {{ill|sacred sake|ja|神酒|qq=draft}}, {{ill|mochi|ja|餅|qq=draft}}, and cooked rice ({{ill|Onii|ja|御飯|qq=draft}}) in the {{ill|Imibiya-den|ja|忌火屋殿|qq=draft}}, and offered before the deities<ref name="sy"/>.
Additionally, during the {{ill|Jingū Shikinen Sengū|ja|神宮式年遷宮|qq=draft}}, one of the key rituals involves the placement of the central sacred pillar ({{ill|Shin-no-Mihashira|ja|心御柱|qq=draft}}), which is temporarily stored in Mishine-no-Mikura<ref>{{Cite news|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20130926070226/http://sankei.jp.msn.com/life/news/130925/trd13092522350017-n1.htm|title=Shin-no-Mihashira Ceremony at Naikū for Jingū Shikinen Sengū|publisher=Sankei Shimbun|work=MSN Sankei News|date=September 25, 2013|accessdate=October 13, 2013}}</ref>.
The shrine building is constructed in the {{ill|Yuitsu-shinmei-zukuri|ja|唯一神明造|qq=draft}} style<ref name="ic"/>. It faces east toward Naikū<ref name="ic"/>. The elevated structure with a {{ill|kirizuma-style|ja|切妻|qq=draft}} roof and decorative {{ill|chigi and katsuogi|ja|千木・鰹木|qq=draft}} resembles the East and West Treasuries of Naikū and the Geheiden<ref name="ss8"/>. Due to its elevated design, a removable staircase is attached during rice retrieval. The {{ill|Gon-negi|ja|権禰宜|qq=draft}} unlocks the door with a sacred key ({{ill|Mikagi|ja|御鑰|qq=draft}}) to access the rice<ref>Yano (2006): pp.111–112</ref>. There are no surrounding {{ill|tamagaki|ja|玉垣|qq=draft}} fences or offering boxes.
== Enshrined deity ==
The enshrined deity is Mishine-no-Mikura-no-Kami (御稲御倉神)<ref name="ic"/>. This deity serves as the {{ill|guardian deity|ja|守護神|qq=draft}} of Mishine-no-Mikura<ref name="ic"/><ref name="gk"/>. During the {{ill|Jingū Shikinen Sengū|ja|神宮式年遷宮|qq=draft}}, when the shrine is rebuilt, Mishine-no-Mikura-no-Kami is temporarily transferred to the Geheiden<ref>Ise Bunka-sha (2013): p.6</ref>.
In the Heian-period text "Daiji Mikagami," the deity is referred to as {{ill|Ukemochi-no-Kami|ja|保食神|1=Ukemochi|qq=draft}}, while in the Kamakura-period "Jinmyō Hisho," it is identified as {{ill|Ukanomitama|ja|ウカノミタマ|qq=draft}}<ref name="gs P155-156">Sakurai (1970): pp.155–156</ref>.
== History ==
It is believed to have existed since ancient times<ref name="gs"/>. Originally, it was part of a complex of four granaries—Tsuki-no-Mikura, Mishio-no-Mikura, Fushiki-no-Mikura, and Mishine-no-Mikura—located west of the outer fence. By the mid-Heian period, these granaries, along with the Geheiden, were moved inside the outer sacred fence<ref>Fukuyama et al. (1975): pp.228–229</ref>. At the time, the structure was built in the {{ill|azekura-zukuri|ja|校倉造|qq=draft}} style without chigi and katsuogi<ref name="ss9">Fukuyama et al. (1975): p.229</ref>. As it was within the grounds of the main sanctuary, it was relocated with each Shikinen Sengū, but all four granaries were abolished by the end of the medieval period<ref name="ss9"/>.
The rice retrieval ceremony was conducted by four officials, including {{ill|negi|ja|禰宜|qq=draft}} and Ōuchi-hito, accompanied by Ōmonoimi (great purification officials)<ref name="gs"/>. Their attire consisted of the same sacred garments used during Naikū rituals, indicating the shrine's significance<ref name="gs"/>. The retrieved rice was milled, steamed, and served in sacred vessels ({{ill|ke|ja|笥|qq=draft}}) before the deities<ref name="gs2"/>. The offerings were dedicated to Naikū, {{ill|Aramatsuri-no-Miya|ja|荒祭宮|qq=draft}}, and {{ill|Takimatsuri-no-Kami|ja|滝deity|qq=draft}}<ref name="gs2"/>.
During the {{ill|Tenshō era|ja|天正|1=Tenshō (Momoyama period)|lt=Tenshō Era|qq=draft}} (1573–1592), Mishine-no-Mikura was restored in the Yuitsu-shinmei-zukuri style northwest of the main sanctuary and has since been rebuilt with each Shikinen Sengū<ref name="ss9"/>. After the Muromachi period, it was also known as the "Sacred Weaving Hall" (御機殿) due to its association with sacred weaving during the {{ill|Kannamesai|ja|神嘗祭|qq=draft}}<ref name="ss9"/>.
Since the Meiji period, Mishine-no-Mikura-no-Kami has been formally enshrined, and in 1889, the shrine’s location was fixed along the path leading to Aramatsuri-no-Miya<ref name="ss9"/>.
== Access ==
; To Ujibashi Bridge * About 15 minutes by {{ill|Mie Kotsu|ja|三重交通|qq=draft}} bus from the south exit (JR side) of {{ill|Ise Station|ja|伊勢市駅|qq=draft}} on the {{ill|JR Sangū Line|ja|参宮線|qq=draft}} or from {{ill|Ujiyamada Station|ja|Ujiyamada Station|qq=draft}} on the Kintetsu Yamada and Toba Lines, alighting at the final stop "Naikū-mae"<ref name="mkr">Mie Prefecture Tourism Federation, "[http://www.kankomie.or.jp/spot/detail_2937.html Ise Jingu (Naikū) Tourist Info - Visit Mie]" (Accessed October 12, 2013)</ref>. Also accessible via bus from {{ill|Isuzugawa Station|ja|五十鈴川駅|qq=draft}} on the Kintetsu Toba Line. * About 5 minutes south via {{ill|Mie Prefectural Route 32|ja|三重県道32号伊勢磯部線|qq=draft}} (Mikimoto Road) from {{ill|Ise-nishi Interchange|ja|伊勢西インターチェンジ|qq=draft}} on the {{ill|Ise Expressway|ja|伊勢自動車道|qq=draft}}, or from {{ill|Ise Interchange|ja|伊勢インターチェンジ|qq=draft}} via {{ill|National Route 23 (Japan)|ja|国道23号|qq=draft}}. Parking available nearby<ref name="mkr"/>.
; From Ujibashi Bridge * Proceed along the path from Ujibashi toward the main sanctuary of Naikū, then take the path leading west toward Aramatsuri-no-Miya, where Mishine-no-Mikura is enshrined<ref name="ic"/>. The nearby Geheiden can also be found here<ref name="ic"/>.

ISBN 978-4-900759-37-4 * 伊勢文化舎 編『いせびとニュース第12号』残暑号、伊勢文化舎・伊勢市観光協会・おかげ参り推進委員会、平成25th year 8月17日、8p. * 宇治山田市役所 編『宇治山田市史 上巻』宇治山田市役所、昭和4th year 1月20日、862p. * [[学研パブリッシング]]『伊勢神宮に行こう』Gakken Mook神社紀行セレクションvol.1、[[薗田稔]]監修、学研マーケティング、2013rd year 7月4日、82p. ISBN 978-4-05-610047-1 * [[櫻井勝之進]]『伊勢神宮』[[学生社]]、昭和45th year 10月20日重版、251p. * [[福山敏男]]・[[稲垣栄三|稲垣榮三]]・村瀬美樹・胡麻鶴醇之『神宮―第六十回神宮式年遷宮―』[[小学館]]、昭和50th year 4月20日、246p. * 矢野憲一『伊勢神宮―知られざる杜のうち』角川選書402、[[角川学芸出版]]、平成18th year 11月10日、270p. ISBN 4-04-703402-9

{{Shrine |Name = Mishine no Mikura |religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Mishine no Mikura.jpg|270px]] |location = {{ill|Mie Prefecture|ja|三重県|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Ise, Mie|ja|伊勢市|qq=draft}}, Ujikan-cho 1, within the precincts of the Inner Shrine |Latitude Degrees = 34 | Latitude Minutes = 27 | Latitude Seconds = 18.8 |Longitude Degrees = 136 | Longitude Minutes = 43 | Longitude Seconds = 28.7 |Map = Japan Mie |Label Position = bottom |deity = Mishine no Mikura no Kami |type = {{ill|Kōtai Jingū|ja|皇大神宮|1=Kōtai Jingū|2=ja|3=皇大神宮|4=zh|5=皇大神宮|qq=draft}} auxiliary shrine |Established = Ancient times |Main Hall = {{ill|Yuitsu Shimmei-zukuri|ja|唯一神明造|qq=draft}} |Other Names = |Pilgrimage Sites = |festival = |Rituals = {{ill|Kannamesai|ja|神嘗祭|qq=draft}} }}
'''Mishine no Mikura''' (御稲御倉, also read as *Gotō no Mikura*<ref name="ss8">Fukuyama et al. (1975): p. 228</ref>) is an auxiliary shrine under the jurisdiction of {{ill|Kōtai Jingū|ja|皇大神宮|1=Kōtai Jingū|2=ja|3=皇大神宮|4=zh|5=皇大神宮|qq=draft}} (the Inner Shrine) of {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮|qq=draft}}. It is also referred to as '''Mishine no Mikura no Kami''' (御稲御倉神)<ref name="ic">Ise Bunka-sha, ed. (2008): p. 114</ref><ref>Ujiyamada City Office, ed. (1929): p. 16</ref><ref name="gs2">Sakurai (1970): p. 156</ref>. The shrine is located within the precincts of the Inner Shrine, along the path leading to {{ill|Aramatsuri-no-Miya|ja|荒祭宮|qq=draft}}<ref name="ic"/>.
Although small, it features the same {{ill|Yuitsu Shimmei-zukuri|ja|唯一神明造|qq=draft}} architectural style as the Inner Shrine<ref name="ic"/><ref name="gk">Gakken Publishing (2013): p. 56</ref>.

The elevated structure with a {{ill|kirizuma-style|ja|切妻|qq=draft}} roof and decorative {{ill|chigi and katsuogi|ja|千木・鰹木|qq=draft}} resembles the East and West Treasuries of Naikū and the Geheiden<ref name="ss8"/>. Due to its elevated design, a removable staircase is attached during rice retrieval. There are no surrounding {{ill|tamagaki|ja|玉垣|qq=draft}} fences or offering boxes.

In the Heian-period text "Daiji Mikagami," the deity is referred to as {{ill|Ukemochi-no-Kami|ja|保食神|1=Ukemochi|qq=draft}}, while in the Kamakura-period "Jinmyō Hisho," it is identified as {{ill|Ukanomitama|ja|ウカノミタマ|qq=draft}}<ref name="gs P155-156">Sakurai (1970): pp.155–156</ref>.

It is believed to have existed since ancient times<ref name="gs"/>. ; To Ujibashi Bridge * About 15 minutes by {{ill|Mie Kotsu|ja|三重交通|qq=draft}} bus from the south exit (JR side) of {{ill|Ise Station|ja|伊勢市駅|qq=draft}} on the {{ill|JR Sangū Line|ja|参宮線|qq=draft}} or from {{ill|Ujiyamada Station|ja|Ujiyamada Station|qq=draft}} on the Kintetsu Yamada and Toba Lines, alighting at the final stop "Naikū-mae"<ref name="mkr">Mie Prefecture Tourism Federation, "[http://www.kankomie.or.jp/spot/detail_2937.html Ise Jingu (Naikū) Tourist Info - Visit Mie]" (Accessed October 12, 2013)</ref>. Also accessible via bus from {{ill|Isuzugawa Station|ja|五十鈴川駅|qq=draft}} on the Kintetsu Toba Line. * About 5 minutes south via {{ill|Mie Prefectural Route 32|ja|三重県道32号伊勢磯部線|qq=draft}} (Mikimoto Road) from {{ill|Ise-nishi Interchange|ja|伊勢西インターチェンジ|qq=draft}} on the {{ill|Ise Expressway|ja|伊勢自動車道|qq=draft}}, or from {{ill|Ise Interchange|ja|伊勢インターチェンジ|qq=draft}} via {{ill|National Route 23 (Japan)|ja|国道23号|qq=draft}}. revision.text:
{{Shrine |Name = Mishine no Mikura |religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Mishine no Mikura.jpg|270px]] |location = {{ill|Mie Prefecture|ja|三重県|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Ise, Mie|ja|伊勢市|qq=draft}}, Ujikan-cho 1, within the precincts of the Inner Shrine |Latitude Degrees = 34 | Latitude Minutes = 27 | Latitude Seconds = 18.8 |Longitude Degrees = 136 | Longitude Minutes = 43 | Longitude Seconds = 28.7 |Map = Japan Mie |Label Position = bottom |deity = Mishine no Mikura no Kami |type = {{ill|Kōtai Jingū|ja|皇大神宮|1=Kōtai Jingū|2=ja|3=皇大神宮|4=zh|5=皇大神宮|qq=draft}} auxiliary shrine |Established = Ancient times |Main Hall = {{ill|Yuitsu Shimmei-zukuri|ja|唯一神明造|qq=draft}} |Other Names = |Pilgrimage Sites = |festival = |Rituals = {{ill|Kannamesai|ja|神嘗祭|qq=draft}} }}
'''Mishine no Mikura''' (御稲御倉, also read as *Gotō no Mikura*<ref name="ss8">Fukuyama et al. (1975): p. 228</ref>) is an auxiliary shrine under the jurisdiction of {{ill|Kōtai Jingū|ja|皇大神宮|1=Kōtai Jingū|2=ja|3=皇大神宮|4=zh|5=皇大神宮|qq=draft}} (the Inner Shrine) of {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮|qq=draft}}. It is also referred to as '''Mishine no Mikura no Kami''' (御稲御倉神)<ref name="ic">Ise Bunka-sha, ed. (2008): p. 114</ref><ref>Ujiyamada City Office, ed. (1929): p. 16</ref><ref name="gs2">Sakurai (1970): p. 156</ref>. The shrine is located within the precincts of the Inner Shrine, along the path leading to {{ill|Aramatsuri-no-Miya|ja|荒祭宮|qq=draft}}<ref name="ic"/>.
Although small, it features the same {{ill|Yuitsu Shimmei-zukuri|ja|唯一神明造|qq=draft}} architectural style as the Inner Shrine<ref name="ic"/><ref name="gk">Gakken Publishing (2013): p. 56</ref>.
== Overview ==
Located in {{ill|Ujikanda-chō|ja|宇治館町|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Ise City|ja|伊勢市|qq=draft}}, Mie Prefecture, this shrine stands northwest of the main sanctuary (Shōgu) within the precincts of the {{ill|Naikū|ja|内宮|qq=draft}}<ref name="gk"/>. It Ranks 6th among the 30 shrines under the jurisdiction of Naikū. Each time a festival is held at the main sanctuary of Naikū, a ritual is also conducted at the {{ill|Mishine-no-Mikura|ja|御稲御倉|qq=draft}}<ref name="ic"/>. To the north of Mishine-no-Mikura is the {{ill|Geheiden|ja|外幣殿|qq=draft}}<ref name="ss8"/>.
The {{ill|rice|ja|イネ|qq=draft}} harvested from the sacred fields of Ise Jingu is presented as {{ill|nuiho|ja|抜穂|qq=draft}} and stored in Mishine-no-Mikura<ref name="ic"/>. This rice is later retrieved in accordance with Naikū’s rituals and offered as {{ill|Ōmikue|ja|大御饌|qq=draft}} to {{ill|Amaterasu|ja|天照大神|1=Amaterasu|lt=Amaterasu Ōmikami|qq=draft}}<ref name="ic"/><ref name="gk"/>. Essentially, it functions as the granary of Naikū<ref name="gk"/>. Following ancient customs, Shinto priests dressed in traditional garments deliver the rice to the Mikura<ref name="gs">Sakurai (1970): p.155</ref>.
The act of retrieving rice from the Mikura is called either {{ill|Gotō Hōge|ja|御稲奉下|qq=draft}} (Gotō Hōge<ref name="gs"/>, Mishine Hōge<ref name="sy">Yano (2006): p.111</ref>). It is carried out by priests who have undergone purification rites the day before<ref name="gs"/>. After retrieval, the rice undergoes threshing, dehusking, and polishing to about 70%. It is then prepared into {{ill|sacred sake|ja|神酒|qq=draft}}, {{ill|mochi|ja|餅|qq=draft}}, and cooked rice ({{ill|Onii|ja|御飯|qq=draft}}) in the {{ill|Imibiya-den|ja|忌火屋殿|qq=draft}}, and offered before the deities<ref name="sy"/>.
Additionally, during the {{ill|Jingū Shikinen Sengū|ja|神宮式年遷宮|qq=draft}}, one of the key rituals involves the placement of the central sacred pillar ({{ill|Shin-no-Mihashira|ja|心御柱|qq=draft}}), which is temporarily stored in Mishine-no-Mikura<ref>{{Cite news|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20130926070226/http://sankei.jp.msn.com/life/news/130925/trd13092522350017-n1.htm|title=Shin-no-Mihashira Ceremony at Naikū for Jingū Shikinen Sengū|publisher=Sankei Shimbun|work=MSN Sankei News|date=September 25, 2013|accessdate=October 13, 2013}}</ref>.
The shrine building is constructed in the {{ill|Yuitsu-shinmei-zukuri|ja|唯一神明造|qq=draft}} style<ref name="ic"/>. It faces east toward Naikū<ref name="ic"/>. The elevated structure with a {{ill|kirizuma-style|ja|切妻|qq=draft}} roof and decorative {{ill|chigi and katsuogi|ja|千木・鰹木|qq=draft}} resembles the East and West Treasuries of Naikū and the Geheiden<ref name="ss8"/>. Due to its elevated design, a removable staircase is attached during rice retrieval. The {{ill|Gon-negi|ja|権禰宜|qq=draft}} unlocks the door with a sacred key ({{ill|Mikagi|ja|御鑰|qq=draft}}) to access the rice<ref>Yano (2006): pp.111–112</ref>. There are no surrounding {{ill|tamagaki|ja|玉垣|qq=draft}} fences or offering boxes.
== Enshrined deity ==
The enshrined deity is Mishine-no-Mikura-no-Kami (御稲御倉神)<ref name="ic"/>. This deity serves as the {{ill|guardian deity|ja|守護神|qq=draft}} of Mishine-no-Mikura<ref name="ic"/><ref name="gk"/>. During the {{ill|Jingū Shikinen Sengū|ja|神宮式年遷宮|qq=draft}}, when the shrine is rebuilt, Mishine-no-Mikura-no-Kami is temporarily transferred to the Geheiden<ref>Ise Bunka-sha (2013): p.6</ref>.
In the Heian-period text "Daiji Mikagami," the deity is referred to as {{ill|Ukemochi-no-Kami|ja|保食神|1=Ukemochi|qq=draft}}, while in the Kamakura-period "Jinmyō Hisho," it is identified as {{ill|Ukanomitama|ja|ウカノミタマ|qq=draft}}<ref name="gs P155-156">Sakurai (1970): pp.155–156</ref>.
== History ==
It is believed to have existed since ancient times<ref name="gs"/>. Originally, it was part of a complex of four granaries—Tsuki-no-Mikura, Mishio-no-Mikura, Fushiki-no-Mikura, and Mishine-no-Mikura—located west of the outer fence. By the mid-Heian period, these granaries, along with the Geheiden, were moved inside the outer sacred fence<ref>Fukuyama et al. (1975): pp.228–229</ref>. At the time, the structure was built in the {{ill|azekura-zukuri|ja|校倉造|qq=draft}} style without chigi and katsuogi<ref name="ss9">Fukuyama et al. (1975): p.229</ref>. As it was within the grounds of the main sanctuary, it was relocated with each Shikinen Sengū, but all four granaries were abolished by the end of the medieval period<ref name="ss9"/>.
The rice retrieval ceremony was conducted by four officials, including {{ill|negi|ja|禰宜|qq=draft}} and Ōuchi-hito, accompanied by Ōmonoimi (great purification officials)<ref name="gs"/>. Their attire consisted of the same sacred garments used during Naikū rituals, indicating the shrine's significance<ref name="gs"/>. The retrieved rice was milled, steamed, and served in sacred vessels ({{ill|ke|ja|笥|qq=draft}}) before the deities<ref name="gs2"/>. The offerings were dedicated to Naikū, {{ill|Aramatsuri-no-Miya|ja|荒祭宮|qq=draft}}, and {{ill|Takimatsuri-no-Kami|ja|滝deity|qq=draft}}<ref name="gs2"/>.
During the {{ill|Tenshō era|ja|天正|1=Tenshō (Momoyama period)|lt=Tenshō Era|qq=draft}} (1573–1592), Mishine-no-Mikura was restored in the Yuitsu-shinmei-zukuri style northwest of the main sanctuary and has since been rebuilt with each Shikinen Sengū<ref name="ss9"/>. After the Muromachi period, it was also known as the "Sacred Weaving Hall" (御機殿) due to its association with sacred weaving during the {{ill|Kannamesai|ja|神嘗祭|qq=draft}}<ref name="ss9"/>.
Since the Meiji period, Mishine-no-Mikura-no-Kami has been formally enshrined, and in 1889, the shrine’s location was fixed along the path leading to Aramatsuri-no-Miya<ref name="ss9"/>.
== Access ==
; To Ujibashi Bridge * About 15 minutes by {{ill|Mie Kotsu|ja|三重交通|qq=draft}} bus from the south exit (JR side) of {{ill|Ise Station|ja|伊勢市駅|qq=draft}} on the {{ill|JR Sangū Line|ja|参宮線|qq=draft}} or from {{ill|Ujiyamada Station|ja|Ujiyamada Station|qq=draft}} on the Kintetsu Yamada and Toba Lines, alighting at the final stop "Naikū-mae"<ref name="mkr">Mie Prefecture Tourism Federation, "[http://www.kankomie.or.jp/spot/detail_2937.html Ise Jingu (Naikū) Tourist Info - Visit Mie]" (Accessed October 12, 2013)</ref>. Also accessible via bus from {{ill|Isuzugawa Station|ja|五十鈴川駅|qq=draft}} on the Kintetsu Toba Line. * About 5 minutes south via {{ill|Mie Prefectural Route 32|ja|三重県道32号伊勢磯部線|qq=draft}} (Mikimoto Road) from {{ill|Ise-nishi Interchange|ja|伊勢西インターチェンジ|qq=draft}} on the {{ill|Ise Expressway|ja|伊勢自動車道|qq=draft}}, or from {{ill|Ise Interchange|ja|伊勢インターチェンジ|qq=draft}} via {{ill|National Route 23 (Japan)|ja|国道23号|qq=draft}}. Parking available nearby<ref name="mkr"/>.
; From Ujibashi Bridge * Proceed along the path from Ujibashi toward the main sanctuary of Naikū, then take the path leading west toward Aramatsuri-no-Miya, where Mishine-no-Mikura is enshrined<ref name="ic"/>. The nearby Geheiden can also be found here<ref name="ic"/>.
== References ==

ISBN 978-4-900759-37-4 * 伊勢文化舎 編『いせびとニュース第12号』残暑号、伊勢文化舎・伊勢市観光協会・おかげ参り推進委員会、平成25th year 8月17日、8p. * 宇治山田市役所 編『宇治山田市史 上巻』宇治山田市役所、昭和4th year 1月20日、862p. * [[学研パブリッシング]]『伊勢神宮に行こう』Gakken Mook神社紀行セレクションvol.1、[[薗田稔]]監修、学研マーケティング、2013rd year 7月4日、82p. ISBN 978-4-05-610047-1 * [[櫻井勝之進]]『伊勢神宮』[[学生社]]、昭和45th year 10月20日重版、251p. * [[福山敏男]]・[[稲垣栄三|稲垣榮三]]・村瀬美樹・胡麻鶴醇之『神宮―第六十回神宮式年遷宮―』[[小学館]]、昭和50th year 4月20日、246p. * 矢野憲一『伊勢神宮―知られざる杜のうち』角川選書402、[[角川学芸出版]]、平成18th year 11月10日、270p. ISBN 4-04-703402-9

Although small, it features the same {{ill|Yuitsu Shimmei-zukuri|ja|唯一神明造|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yuitsu Shimmei-zukuri|qq=}} architectural style as the Inner Shrine<ref name="ic"/><ref name="gk">Gakken Publishing (2013): p. 56</ref>.

Located in {{ill|Ujikanda-chō|ja|宇治館町|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ujikanda-chō|qq=}}, {{ill|Ise City|ja|伊勢市|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise City|qq=}}, Mie Prefecture, this shrine stands northwest of the main sanctuary (Shōgu) within the precincts of the {{ill|Naikū|ja|内宮|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Naikū|qq=}}<ref name="gk"/>. Each time a festival is held at the main sanctuary of Naikū, a ritual is also conducted at the {{ill|Mishine-no-Mikura|ja|御稲御倉|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mishine-no-Mikura|qq=}}<ref name="ic"/>. To the north of Mishine-no-Mikura is the {{ill|Geheiden|ja|外幣殿|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Geheiden|qq=}}<ref name="ss8"/>.

The act of retrieving rice from the Mikura is called either {{ill|Gotō Hōge|ja|御稲奉下|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gotō Hōge|qq=}} (Gotō Hōge<ref name="gs"/>, Mishine Hōge<ref name="sy">Yano (2006): p.111</ref>). It is carried out by priests who have undergone purification rites the day before<ref name="gs"/>. After retrieval, the rice undergoes threshing, dehusking, and polishing to about 70%. It is then prepared into {{ill|sacred sake|ja|神酒|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/sacred sake|qq=}}, {{ill|mochi|ja|餅|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/mochi|qq=}}, and cooked rice ({{ill|Onii|ja|御飯|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Onii|qq=}}) in the {{ill|Imibiya-den|ja|忌火屋殿|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Imibiya-den|qq=}}, and offered before the deities<ref name="sy"/>.

The shrine building is constructed in the {{ill|Yuitsu-shinmei-zukuri|ja|唯一神明造|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yuitsu-shinmei-zukuri|qq=}} style<ref name="ic"/>. It faces east toward Naikū<ref name="ic"/>. The elevated structure with a {{ill|kirizuma-style|ja|切妻|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/kirizuma-style|qq=}} roof and decorative {{ill|chigi and katsuogi|ja|千木・鰹木|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/chigi and katsuogi|qq=}} resembles the East and West Treasuries of Naikū and the Geheiden<ref name="ss8"/>. There are no surrounding {{ill|tamagaki|ja|玉垣|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/tamagaki|qq=}} fences or offering boxes.

The enshrined deity is Mishine-no-Mikura-no-Kami (御稲御倉神)<ref name="ic"/>. This deity serves as the {{ill|guardian deity|ja|守護神|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/guardian deity|qq=}} of Mishine-no-Mikura<ref name="ic"/><ref name="gk"/>. (1975): p.229</ref>. Since the Meiji period, Mishine-no-Mikura-no-Kami has been formally enshrined, and in 1889, the shrine’s location was fixed along the path leading to Aramatsuri-no-Miya<ref name="ss9"/>.
== Access ==
; To Ujibashi Bridge * About 15 minutes by {{ill|Mie Kotsu|ja|三重交通|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mie Kotsu|qq=}} bus from the south exit (JR side) of {{ill|Ise Station|ja|伊勢市駅|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise Station|qq=}} on the {{ill|JR Sangū Line|ja|参宮線|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/JR Sangū Line|qq=}} or from {{ill|Ujiyamada Station|ja|Ujiyamada Station|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ujiyamada Station|qq=}} on the Kintetsu Yamada and Toba Lines, alighting at the final stop "Naikū-mae"<ref name="mkr">Mie Prefecture Tourism Federation, "[http://www.kankomie.or.jp/spot/detail_2937.html Ise Jingu (Naikū) Tourist Info - Visit Mie]" (Accessed October 12, 2013)</ref>. Also accessible via bus from {{ill|Isuzugawa Station|ja|五十鈴川駅|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Isuzugawa Station|qq=}} on the Kintetsu Toba Line. * About 5 minutes south via {{ill|Mie Prefectural Route 32|ja|三重県道32号伊勢磯部線|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mie Prefectural Route 32|qq=}} (Mikimoto Road) from {{ill|Ise-nishi Interchange|ja|伊勢西インターチェンジ|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise-nishi Interchange|qq=}} on the {{ill|Ise Expressway|ja|伊勢自動車道|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise Expressway|qq=}}, or from {{ill|Ise Interchange|ja|伊勢インターチェンジ|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise Interchange|qq=}} via {{ill|National Route 23 (Japan)|ja|国道23号|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/National Route 23 (Japan)|qq=}}. Parking available nearby<ref name="mkr"/>.
; From Ujibashi Bridge * Proceed along the path from Ujibashi toward the main sanctuary of Naikū, then take the path leading west toward Aramatsuri-no-Miya, where Mishine-no-Mikura is enshrined<ref name="ic"/>. The nearby Geheiden can also be found here<ref name="ic"/>.

revision.comment: Copying from [[Category:125 Shrines of Ise]] to [[Category:Shinto shrines in Mie Prefecture]] using [[c:Help:Cat-a-lot|Cat-a-lot]]
revision.text:
{{Shrine |Name = Mishine no Mikura |religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Mishine no Mikura.jpg|270px]] |location = {{ill|Mie Prefecture|ja|三重県|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mie Prefecture|qq=}}, {{ill|Ise, Mie|ja|伊勢市|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise, Mie|qq=}}, Ujikan-cho 1, within the precincts of the Inner Shrine |Latitude Degrees = 34 | Latitude Minutes = 27 | Latitude Seconds = 18.8 |Longitude Degrees = 136 | Longitude Minutes = 43 | Longitude Seconds = 28.7 |Map = Japan Mie |Label Position = bottom |deity = Mishine no Mikura no Kami |type = {{ill|Kōtai Jingū|ja|皇大神宮|1=Kōtai Jingū|2=ja|3=皇大神宮|4=zh|5=皇大神宮|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōtai Jingū|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōtai Jingū|qq=}} auxiliary shrine |Established = Ancient times |Main Hall = {{ill|Yuitsu Shimmei-zukuri|ja|唯一神明造|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yuitsu Shimmei-zukuri|qq=}} |Other Names = |Pilgrimage Sites = |festival = |Rituals = {{ill|Kannamesai|ja|神嘗祭|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kannamesai|qq=}} }}
'''Mishine no Mikura''' (御稲御倉, also read as *Gotō no Mikura*<ref name="ss8">Fukuyama et al. (1975): p. 228</ref>) is an auxiliary shrine under the jurisdiction of {{ill|Kōtai Jingū|ja|皇大神宮|1=Kōtai Jingū|2=ja|3=皇大神宮|4=zh|5=皇大神宮|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōtai Jingū|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōtai Jingū|qq=}} (the Inner Shrine) of {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise Grand Shrine|qq=}}. It is also referred to as '''Mishine no Mikura no Kami''' (御稲御倉神)<ref name="ic">Ise Bunka-sha, ed. (2008): p. 114</ref><ref>Ujiyamada City Office, ed. (1929): p. 16</ref><ref name="gs2">Sakurai (1970): p. 156</ref>. The shrine is located within the precincts of the Inner Shrine, along the path leading to {{ill|Aramatsuri-no-Miya|ja|荒祭宮|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Aramatsuri-no-Miya|qq=}}<ref name="ic"/>.
Although small, it features the same {{ill|Yuitsu Shimmei-zukuri|ja|唯一神明造|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yuitsu Shimmei-zukuri|qq=}} architectural style as the Inner Shrine<ref name="ic"/><ref name="gk">Gakken Publishing (2013): p. 56</ref>.
== Overview ==
Located in {{ill|Ujikanda-chō|ja|宇治館町|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ujikanda-chō|qq=}}, {{ill|Ise City|ja|伊勢市|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise City|qq=}}, Mie Prefecture, this shrine stands northwest of the main sanctuary (Shōgu) within the precincts of the {{ill|Naikū|ja|内宮|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Naikū|qq=}}<ref name="gk"/>. It Ranks 6th among the 30 shrines under the jurisdiction of Naikū. Each time a festival is held at the main sanctuary of Naikū, a ritual is also conducted at the {{ill|Mishine-no-Mikura|ja|御稲御倉|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mishine-no-Mikura|qq=}}<ref name="ic"/>. To the north of Mishine-no-Mikura is the {{ill|Geheiden|ja|外幣殿|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Geheiden|qq=}}<ref name="ss8"/>.
The {{ill|rice|ja|イネ|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/rice|qq=}} harvested from the sacred fields of Ise Jingu is presented as {{ill|nuiho|ja|抜穂|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/nuiho|qq=}} and stored in Mishine-no-Mikura<ref name="ic"/>. This rice is later retrieved in accordance with Naikū’s rituals and offered as {{ill|Ōmikue|ja|大御饌|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōmikue|qq=}} to {{ill|Amaterasu|ja|天照大神|1=Amaterasu|lt=Amaterasu Ōmikami|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amaterasu|13=User:Immanuelle/Amaterasu|qq=}}<ref name="ic"/><ref name="gk"/>. Essentially, it functions as the granary of Naikū<ref name="gk"/>. Following ancient customs, Shinto priests dressed in traditional garments deliver the rice to the Mikura<ref name="gs">Sakurai (1970): p.155</ref>.
The act of retrieving rice from the Mikura is called either {{ill|Gotō Hōge|ja|御稲奉下|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gotō Hōge|qq=}} (Gotō Hōge<ref name="gs"/>, Mishine Hōge<ref name="sy">Yano (2006): p.111</ref>). It is carried out by priests who have undergone purification rites the day before<ref name="gs"/>. After retrieval, the rice undergoes threshing, dehusking, and polishing to about 70%. It is then prepared into {{ill|sacred sake|ja|神酒|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/sacred sake|qq=}}, {{ill|mochi|ja|餅|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/mochi|qq=}}, and cooked rice ({{ill|Onii|ja|御飯|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Onii|qq=}}) in the {{ill|Imibiya-den|ja|忌火屋殿|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Imibiya-den|qq=}}, and offered before the deities<ref name="sy"/>.
Additionally, during the {{ill|Jingū Shikinen Sengū|ja|神宮式年遷宮|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jingū Shikinen Sengū|qq=}}, one of the key rituals involves the placement of the central sacred pillar ({{ill|Shin-no-Mihashira|ja|心御柱|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shin-no-Mihashira|qq=}}), which is temporarily stored in Mishine-no-Mikura<ref>{{Cite news|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20130926070226/http://sankei.jp.msn.com/life/news/130925/trd13092522350017-n1.htm|title=Shin-no-Mihashira Ceremony at Naikū for Jingū Shikinen Sengū|publisher=Sankei Shimbun|work=MSN Sankei News|date=September 25, 2013|accessdate=October 13, 2013}}</ref>.
The shrine building is constructed in the {{ill|Yuitsu-shinmei-zukuri|ja|唯一神明造|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yuitsu-shinmei-zukuri|qq=}} style<ref name="ic"/>. It faces east toward Naikū<ref name="ic"/>. The elevated structure with a {{ill|kirizuma-style|ja|切妻|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/kirizuma-style|qq=}} roof and decorative {{ill|chigi and katsuogi|ja|千木・鰹木|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/chigi and katsuogi|qq=}} resembles the East and West Treasuries of Naikū and the Geheiden<ref name="ss8"/>. Due to its elevated design, a removable staircase is attached during rice retrieval. The {{ill|Gon-negi|ja|権禰宜|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gon-negi|qq=}} unlocks the door with a sacred key ({{ill|Mikagi|ja|御鑰|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mikagi|qq=}}) to access the rice<ref>Yano (2006): pp.111–112</ref>. There are no surrounding {{ill|tamagaki|ja|玉垣|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/tamagaki|qq=}} fences or offering boxes.
== Enshrined deity ==
The enshrined deity is Mishine-no-Mikura-no-Kami (御稲御倉神)<ref name="ic"/>. This deity serves as the {{ill|guardian deity|ja|守護神|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/guardian deity|qq=}} of Mishine-no-Mikura<ref name="ic"/><ref name="gk"/>. During the {{ill|Jingū Shikinen Sengū|ja|神宮式年遷宮|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jingū Shikinen Sengū|qq=}}, when the shrine is rebuilt, Mishine-no-Mikura-no-Kami is temporarily transferred to the Geheiden<ref>Ise Bunka-sha (2013): p.6</ref>.
In the Heian-period text "Daiji Mikagami," the deity is referred to as {{ill|Ukemochi-no-Kami|ja|保食神|1=Ukemochi|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ukemochi-no-Kami|13=User:Immanuelle/Ukemochi|qq=}}, while in the Kamakura-period "Jinmyō Hisho," it is identified as {{ill|Ukanomitama|ja|ウカノミタマ|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ukanomitama|qq=}}<ref name="gs P155-156">Sakurai (1970): pp.155–156</ref>.
== History ==
It is believed to have existed since ancient times<ref name="gs"/>. Originally, it was part of a complex of four granaries—Tsuki-no-Mikura, Mishio-no-Mikura, Fushiki-no-Mikura, and Mishine-no-Mikura—located west of the outer fence. By the mid-Heian period, these granaries, along with the Geheiden, were moved inside the outer sacred fence<ref>Fukuyama et al. (1975): pp.228–229</ref>. At the time, the structure was built in the {{ill|azekura-zukuri|ja|校倉造|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/azekura-zukuri|qq=}} style without chigi and katsuogi<ref name="ss9">Fukuyama et al. (1975): p.229</ref>. As it was within the grounds of the main sanctuary, it was relocated with each Shikinen Sengū, but all four granaries were abolished by the end of the medieval period<ref name="ss9"/>.
The rice retrieval ceremony was conducted by four officials, including {{ill|negi|ja|禰宜|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/negi|qq=}} and Ōuchi-hito, accompanied by Ōmonoimi (great purification officials)<ref name="gs"/>. Their attire consisted of the same sacred garments used during Naikū rituals, indicating the shrine's significance<ref name="gs"/>. The retrieved rice was milled, steamed, and served in sacred vessels ({{ill|ke|ja|笥|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/ke|qq=}}) before the deities<ref name="gs2"/>. The offerings were dedicated to Naikū, {{ill|Aramatsuri-no-Miya|ja|荒祭宮|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Aramatsuri-no-Miya|qq=}}, and {{ill|Takimatsuri-no-Kami|ja|滝deity|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takimatsuri-no-Kami|qq=}}<ref name="gs2"/>.
During the {{ill|Tenshō era|ja|天正|1=Tenshō (Momoyama period)|lt=Tenshō Era|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenshō era|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenshō (Momoyama period)|qq=}} (1573–1592), Mishine-no-Mikura was restored in the Yuitsu-shinmei-zukuri style northwest of the main sanctuary and has since been rebuilt with each Shikinen Sengū<ref name="ss9"/>. After the Muromachi period, it was also known as the "Sacred Weaving Hall" (御機殿) due to its association with sacred weaving during the {{ill|Kannamesai|ja|神嘗祭|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kannamesai|qq=}}<ref name="ss9"/>.
Since the Meiji period, Mishine-no-Mikura-no-Kami has been formally enshrined, and in 1889, the shrine’s location was fixed along the path leading to Aramatsuri-no-Miya<ref name="ss9"/>.
== Access ==
; To Ujibashi Bridge * About 15 minutes by {{ill|Mie Kotsu|ja|三重交通|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mie Kotsu|qq=}} bus from the south exit (JR side) of {{ill|Ise Station|ja|伊勢市駅|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise Station|qq=}} on the {{ill|JR Sangū Line|ja|参宮線|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/JR Sangū Line|qq=}} or from {{ill|Ujiyamada Station|ja|Ujiyamada Station|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ujiyamada Station|qq=}} on the Kintetsu Yamada and Toba Lines, alighting at the final stop "Naikū-mae"<ref name="mkr">Mie Prefecture Tourism Federation, "[http://www.kankomie.or.jp/spot/detail_2937.html Ise Jingu (Naikū) Tourist Info - Visit Mie]" (Accessed October 12, 2013)</ref>. Also accessible via bus from {{ill|Isuzugawa Station|ja|五十鈴川駅|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Isuzugawa Station|qq=}} on the Kintetsu Toba Line. * About 5 minutes south via {{ill|Mie Prefectural Route 32|ja|三重県道32号伊勢磯部線|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mie Prefectural Route 32|qq=}} (Mikimoto Road) from {{ill|Ise-nishi Interchange|ja|伊勢西インターチェンジ|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise-nishi Interchange|qq=}} on the {{ill|Ise Expressway|ja|伊勢自動車道|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise Expressway|qq=}}, or from {{ill|Ise Interchange|ja|伊勢インターチェンジ|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise Interchange|qq=}} via {{ill|National Route 23 (Japan)|ja|国道23号|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/National Route 23 (Japan)|qq=}}. Parking available nearby<ref name="mkr"/>.
; From Ujibashi Bridge * Proceed along the path from Ujibashi toward the main sanctuary of Naikū, then take the path leading west toward Aramatsuri-no-Miya, where Mishine-no-Mikura is enshrined<ref name="ic"/>. The nearby Geheiden can also be found here<ref name="ic"/>.

{{Shrine |Name = Mishine no Mikura |religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Mishine no Mikura.jpg|270px]] |location = {{ill|Mie Prefecture|ja|三重県|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mie Prefecture|qq=}}, {{ill|Ise, Mie|ja|伊勢市|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise, Mie|qq=}}, Ujikan-cho 1, within the precincts of the Inner Shrine |Latitude Degrees = 34 | Latitude Minutes = 27 | Latitude Seconds = 18.8 |Longitude Degrees = 136 | Longitude Minutes = 43 | Longitude Seconds = 28.7 |Map = Japan Mie |Label Position = bottom |deity = Mishine no Mikura no Kami |type = {{ill|Kōtai Jingū|ja|皇大神宮|1=Kōtai Jingū|2=ja|3=皇大神宮|4=zh|5=皇大神宮|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōtai Jingū|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōtai Jingū|qq=}} auxiliary shrine |Established = Ancient times |Main Hall = {{ill|Yuitsu Shimmei-zukuri|ja|唯一神明造|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yuitsu Shimmei-zukuri|qq=}} |Other Names = |Pilgrimage Sites = |festival = |Rituals = {{ill|Kannamesai|ja|神嘗祭|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kannamesai|qq=}} }}
'''Mishine no Mikura''' (御稲御倉, also read as *Gotō no Mikura*<ref name="ss8">Fukuyama et al. (1975): p. 228</ref>) is an auxiliary shrine under the jurisdiction of {{ill|Kōtai Jingū|ja|皇大神宮|1=Kōtai Jingū|2=ja|3=皇大神宮|4=zh|5=皇大神宮|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōtai Jingū|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōtai Jingū|qq=}} (the Inner Shrine) of {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise Grand Shrine|qq=}}. It is also referred to as '''Mishine no Mikura no Kami''' (御稲御倉神)<ref name="ic">Ise Bunka-sha, ed. (2008): p. 114</ref><ref>Ujiyamada City Office, ed. (1929): p. 16</ref><ref name="gs2">Sakurai (1970): p. 156</ref>. The shrine is located within the precincts of the Inner Shrine, along the path leading to {{ill|Aramatsuri-no-Miya|ja|荒祭宮|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Aramatsuri-no-Miya|qq=}}<ref name="ic"/>.
Although small, it features the same {{ill|Yuitsu Shimmei-zukuri|ja|唯一神明造|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yuitsu Shimmei-zukuri|qq=}} architectural style as the Inner Shrine<ref name="ic"/><ref name="gk">Gakken Publishing (2013): p. 56</ref>.
== Overview ==
Located in {{ill|Ujikanda-chō|ja|宇治館町|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ujikanda-chō|qq=}}, {{ill|Ise City|ja|伊勢市|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise City|qq=}}, Mie Prefecture, this shrine stands northwest of the main sanctuary (Shōgu) within the precincts of the {{ill|Naikū|ja|内宮|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Naikū|qq=}}<ref name="gk"/>. It Ranks 6th among the 30 shrines under the jurisdiction of Naikū. Each time a festival is held at the main sanctuary of Naikū, a ritual is also conducted at the {{ill|Mishine-no-Mikura|ja|御稲御倉|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mishine-no-Mikura|qq=}}<ref name="ic"/>. To the north of Mishine-no-Mikura is the {{ill|Geheiden|ja|外幣殿|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Geheiden|qq=}}<ref name="ss8"/>.
The {{ill|rice|ja|イネ|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/rice|qq=}} harvested from the sacred fields of Ise Jingu is presented as {{ill|nuiho|ja|抜穂|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/nuiho|qq=}} and stored in Mishine-no-Mikura<ref name="ic"/>. This rice is later retrieved in accordance with Naikū’s rituals and offered as {{ill|Ōmikue|ja|大御饌|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōmikue|qq=}} to {{ill|Amaterasu|ja|天照大神|1=Amaterasu|lt=Amaterasu Ōmikami|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amaterasu|13=User:Immanuelle/Amaterasu|qq=}}<ref name="ic"/><ref name="gk"/>. Essentially, it functions as the granary of Naikū<ref name="gk"/>. Following ancient customs, Shinto priests dressed in traditional garments deliver the rice to the Mikura<ref name="gs">Sakurai (1970): p.155</ref>.
The act of retrieving rice from the Mikura is called either {{ill|Gotō Hōge|ja|御稲奉下|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gotō Hōge|qq=}} (Gotō Hōge<ref name="gs"/>, Mishine Hōge<ref name="sy">Yano (2006): p.111</ref>). It is carried out by priests who have undergone purification rites the day before<ref name="gs"/>. After retrieval, the rice undergoes threshing, dehusking, and polishing to about 70%. It is then prepared into {{ill|sacred sake|ja|神酒|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/sacred sake|qq=}}, {{ill|mochi|ja|餅|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/mochi|qq=}}, and cooked rice ({{ill|Onii|ja|御飯|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Onii|qq=}}) in the {{ill|Imibiya-den|ja|忌火屋殿|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Imibiya-den|qq=}}, and offered before the deities<ref name="sy"/>.
Additionally, during the {{ill|Jingū Shikinen Sengū|ja|神宮式年遷宮|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jingū Shikinen Sengū|qq=}}, one of the key rituals involves the placement of the central sacred pillar ({{ill|Shin-no-Mihashira|ja|心御柱|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shin-no-Mihashira|qq=}}), which is temporarily stored in Mishine-no-Mikura<ref>{{Cite news|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20130926070226/http://sankei.jp.msn.com/life/news/130925/trd13092522350017-n1.htm|title=Shin-no-Mihashira Ceremony at Naikū for Jingū Shikinen Sengū|publisher=Sankei Shimbun|work=MSN Sankei News|date=September 25, 2013|accessdate=October 13, 2013}}</ref>.
The shrine building is constructed in the {{ill|Yuitsu-shinmei-zukuri|ja|唯一神明造|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yuitsu-shinmei-zukuri|qq=}} style<ref name="ic"/>. It faces east toward Naikū<ref name="ic"/>. The elevated structure with a {{ill|kirizuma-style|ja|切妻|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/kirizuma-style|qq=}} roof and decorative {{ill|chigi and katsuogi|ja|千木・鰹木|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/chigi and katsuogi|qq=}} resembles the East and West Treasuries of Naikū and the Geheiden<ref name="ss8"/>. Due to its elevated design, a removable staircase is attached during rice retrieval. The {{ill|Gon-negi|ja|権禰宜|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gon-negi|qq=}} unlocks the door with a sacred key ({{ill|Mikagi|ja|御鑰|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mikagi|qq=}}) to access the rice<ref>Yano (2006): pp.111–112</ref>. There are no surrounding {{ill|tamagaki|ja|玉垣|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/tamagaki|qq=}} fences or offering boxes.
== Enshrined deity ==
The enshrined deity is Mishine-no-Mikura-no-Kami (御稲御倉神)<ref name="ic"/>. This deity serves as the {{ill|guardian deity|ja|守護神|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/guardian deity|qq=}} of Mishine-no-Mikura<ref name="ic"/><ref name="gk"/>. During the {{ill|Jingū Shikinen Sengū|ja|神宮式年遷宮|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jingū Shikinen Sengū|qq=}}, when the shrine is rebuilt, Mishine-no-Mikura-no-Kami is temporarily transferred to the Geheiden<ref>Ise Bunka-sha (2013): p.6</ref>.
In the Heian-period text "Daiji Mikagami," the deity is referred to as {{ill|Ukemochi-no-Kami|ja|保食神|1=Ukemochi|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ukemochi-no-Kami|13=User:Immanuelle/Ukemochi|qq=}}, while in the Kamakura-period "Jinmyō Hisho," it is identified as {{ill|Ukanomitama|ja|ウカノミタマ|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ukanomitama|qq=}}<ref name="gs P155-156">Sakurai (1970): pp.155–156</ref>.
== History ==
It is believed to have existed since ancient times<ref name="gs"/>. Originally, it was part of a complex of four granaries—Tsuki-no-Mikura, Mishio-no-Mikura, Fushiki-no-Mikura, and Mishine-no-Mikura—located west of the outer fence. By the mid-Heian period, these granaries, along with the Geheiden, were moved inside the outer sacred fence<ref>Fukuyama et al. (1975): pp.228–229</ref>. At the time, the structure was built in the {{ill|azekura-zukuri|ja|校倉造|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/azekura-zukuri|qq=}} style without chigi and katsuogi<ref name="ss9">Fukuyama et al. (1975): p.229</ref>. As it was within the grounds of the main sanctuary, it was relocated with each Shikinen Sengū, but all four granaries were abolished by the end of the medieval period<ref name="ss9"/>.
The rice retrieval ceremony was conducted by four officials, including {{ill|negi|ja|禰宜|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/negi|qq=}} and Ōuchi-hito, accompanied by Ōmonoimi (great purification officials)<ref name="gs"/>. Their attire consisted of the same sacred garments used during Naikū rituals, indicating the shrine's significance<ref name="gs"/>. The retrieved rice was milled, steamed, and served in sacred vessels ({{ill|ke|ja|笥|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/ke|qq=}}) before the deities<ref name="gs2"/>. The offerings were dedicated to Naikū, {{ill|Aramatsuri-no-Miya|ja|荒祭宮|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Aramatsuri-no-Miya|qq=}}, and {{ill|Takimatsuri-no-Kami|ja|滝deity|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takimatsuri-no-Kami|qq=}}<ref name="gs2"/>.
During the {{ill|Tenshō era|ja|天正|1=Tenshō (Momoyama period)|lt=Tenshō Era|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenshō era|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenshō (Momoyama period)|qq=}} (1573–1592), Mishine-no-Mikura was restored in the Yuitsu-shinmei-zukuri style northwest of the main sanctuary and has since been rebuilt with each Shikinen Sengū<ref name="ss9"/>. After the Muromachi period, it was also known as the "Sacred Weaving Hall" (御機殿) due to its association with sacred weaving during the {{ill|Kannamesai|ja|神嘗祭|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kannamesai|qq=}}<ref name="ss9"/>.
Since the Meiji period, Mishine-no-Mikura-no-Kami has been formally enshrined, and in 1889, the shrine’s location was fixed along the path leading to Aramatsuri-no-Miya<ref name="ss9"/>.
== Access ==
; To Ujibashi Bridge * About 15 minutes by {{ill|Mie Kotsu|ja|三重交通|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mie Kotsu|qq=}} bus from the south exit (JR side) of {{ill|Ise Station|ja|伊勢市駅|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise Station|qq=}} on the {{ill|JR Sangū Line|ja|参宮線|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/JR Sangū Line|qq=}} or from {{ill|Ujiyamada Station|ja|Ujiyamada Station|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ujiyamada Station|qq=}} on the Kintetsu Yamada and Toba Lines, alighting at the final stop "Naikū-mae"<ref name="mkr">Mie Prefecture Tourism Federation, "[http://www.kankomie.or.jp/spot/detail_2937.html Ise Jingu (Naikū) Tourist Info - Visit Mie]" (Accessed October 12, 2013)</ref>. Also accessible via bus from {{ill|Isuzugawa Station|ja|五十鈴川駅|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Isuzugawa Station|qq=}} on the Kintetsu Toba Line. * About 5 minutes south via {{ill|Mie Prefectural Route 32|ja|三重県道32号伊勢磯部線|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mie Prefectural Route 32|qq=}} (Mikimoto Road) from {{ill|Ise-nishi Interchange|ja|伊勢西インターチェンジ|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise-nishi Interchange|qq=}} on the {{ill|Ise Expressway|ja|伊勢自動車道|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise Expressway|qq=}}, or from {{ill|Ise Interchange|ja|伊勢インターチェンジ|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise Interchange|qq=}} via {{ill|National Route 23 (Japan)|ja|国道23号|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/National Route 23 (Japan)|qq=}}. Parking available nearby<ref name="mkr"/>.
; From Ujibashi Bridge * Proceed along the path from Ujibashi toward the main sanctuary of Naikū, then take the path leading west toward Aramatsuri-no-Miya, where Mishine-no-Mikura is enshrined<ref name="ic"/>. The nearby Geheiden can also be found here<ref name="ic"/>.
